choices.c: It's passive observation, not active practice. A stronger use would have students identify specific techniques, compare works, or make curatorial decisions inside the virtual museum.

feedback.a: Museum tours can be engaging, but 'appreciate beauty' is a passive, unmeasurable objective. Without a task — something to find, compare, or decide — students are just sightseeing. Add an active task to leverage VR's strengths.
feedback.b: VR can work well for art education! The issue isn't the subject but the objective. Viewing is passive. If students had to identify brushstroke techniques, compare two paintings, or curate a virtual exhibition, VR would add real value.
feedback.c: Exactly. 'Appreciate beauty' is passive and unmeasurable. But if students had a focus task — identify three uses of perspective, compare two artists' techniques, or arrange an exhibition — VR becomes a powerful active learning tool.

cls.instruction: Classify each objective: does it work well in VR, or is it better served without VR?
cls.i1: Perform a fire evacuation procedure in a simulated building
cls.i2: Memorise the names of all 50 US states
cls.i3: Respond to a simulated patient showing signs of stroke
cls.i4: Read and summarise a chapter from a history textbook
cls.i5: Practice assembling a machine following the correct sequence

exercise.instruction: Build a VR lesson plan by selecting the best option at each step

s1.label: Learning Objective
s1.desc: What should students achieve in this VR session?

a.label: Students will explore a VR environment
a.feedback: Exploration is a start, but without a measurable outcome, how do you know they learned?

b.label: Students will identify 3 safety hazards in a virtual lab
b.feedback: Excellent! This is specific (3 hazards), measurable (you can count them), and uses VR's strengths (spatial observation).

c.label: Students will learn about lab safety
c.feedback: This is too vague. How many hazards? What specific behavior proves they "learned"? Add specificity.

s2.label: VR App Selection
s2.desc: Which VR application will you use?

a.label: The newest, most popular VR game
a.desc: Trendy but not aligned with the objective
a.feedback: Popular doesn't mean pedagogically appropriate. Always match the app to your learning objective.

b.label: A virtual lab simulation with interactive hazard scenarios
b.feedback: The app directly supports your objective — students can actually find and interact with safety hazards.

c.label: A 360° video tour of a real lab
c.feedback: A video shows hazards but students can't interact. For an "identify" objective, they need to actively discover.

s3.label: Student Activity
s3.desc: What will students do during the VR session?

a.label: Free exploration — let them discover at their own pace
a.feedback: Some structure helps. Without guidance, students may miss key hazards or get distracted by non-essential elements.

b.label: Watch the teacher demonstrate first, then repeat
b.feedback: This reduces VR's advantage. The whole point is students discovering hazards themselves, not copying a demo.

c.label: Guided investigation with a checklist of areas to inspect
c.feedback: This balances freedom with focus. Students explore actively but have a framework to ensure they cover key areas.

a.label: Exit ticket: students list hazards found and explain why each is dangerous
a.desc: Written reflection linking VR experience to knowledge
a.feedback: Connects the VR experience to demonstrated understanding. Students must recall AND explain — deeper processing.

b.label: Participation grade — if they wore the headset, they pass
b.feedback: Wearing a headset doesn't prove learning. A student could stare at the ceiling the whole time.

c.label: Group discussion about the VR experience
c.feedback: Discussions are valuable but hard to assess individually. Consider combining with a written component.

scenario.context: A colleague shares their VR lesson objective: 'Students will understand the importance of hygiene protocols in a hospital setting.'
scenario.question: What feedback would you give?

choices.a: It's perfect — understanding is a valid learning goal
choices.b: The verb 'understand' is passive and unmeasurable. Rewrite it with an action verb: 'Students will perform a hand hygiene sequence on a virtual patient, following all five WHO steps in the correct order.'
choices.c: It just needs to mention VR — add 'using VR headsets' to the end

feedback.a: 'Understand' is the most common trap in objective writing. You can't observe or measure understanding. VR objectives must describe what students DO — perform, demonstrate, execute — so you can see whether they achieved it.
feedback.b: Perfect feedback. You've replaced a vague verb with a specific action (perform), added context (virtual patient), and included measurable criteria (five WHO steps, correct order). This objective fully leverages VR's strengths.
feedback.c: Adding 'using VR' doesn't fix the core problem. The objective needs an observable action verb. 'Understand' can't be seen or measured — 'perform', 'demonstrate', or 'execute' can. The tool matters less than what students do with it.

c1.context: You need to write a VR objective for a culinary arts class. The topic is knife safety.
c1.question: Which objective is best?

choices.a: Students will demonstrate the correct grip, cutting motion, and safety positioning for three knife types in a virtual kitchen, with zero unsafe hand placements.
choices.b: Students will watch a video about knife safety and understand the risks.
choices.c: Students will learn about proper knife handling techniques.

feedback.a: Excellent. 'Demonstrate' is an action verb. 'Three knife types in a virtual kitchen' sets the context. 'Zero unsafe hand placements' is a measurable criterion. This objective fully leverages VR.
feedback.b: Watching a video is passive — VR's value is in doing, not watching. And 'understand the risks' can't be observed or measured. This objective doesn't need VR at all.
feedback.c: 'Learn about' is as vague as 'understand.' There's no action to observe and no way to measure success. Rewrite with a verb like 'demonstrate,' 'perform,' or 'execute.'

c2.context: A VR objective reads: 'Students will apply the correct triage protocol to categorise five virtual patients by severity, justifying each decision within 2 minutes per patient.'
c2.question: How would you rate this objective?

choices.a: It's too complex — VR objectives should be simpler
choices.b: Strong — it has an action verb (apply), context (five virtual patients), and criteria (correct categorisation, justification, time limit). All three components are present.
choices.c: It's good but the time limit is unnecessary

feedback.a: Complexity is fine when it matches the real task. Triage IS complex — the objective should reflect that. All three components (action + context + criteria) are present and well-defined.
feedback.b: Exactly. This is a model VR objective. 'Apply' = action, 'five virtual patients by severity' = context, 'justifying each decision within 2 minutes' = criteria. It's specific, measurable, and perfectly suited for VR simulation.
feedback.c: The time limit is actually important — it adds realistic pressure and makes the criterion measurable. In real triage, speed matters. Removing it would weaken the objective by losing a key assessment metric.

s1.statement: A strong VR objective uses action verbs like 'perform', 'demonstrate', or 'execute' — not 'understand' or 'learn about'
s1.explanation: Action verbs describe observable behaviour. 'Understand' can't be seen or measured; 'perform' can.

s2.statement: Adding 'in VR' to any objective automatically makes it a good VR objective
s2.explanation: The tool doesn't fix the verb. 'Students will understand X in VR' is still a weak objective. The action verb and measurable criteria matter more than the medium.

s3.statement: A well-written VR objective includes three components: an action, a context, and a success criterion
s3.explanation: Action (what they do), Context (where/under what conditions), and Criterion (how you know it was done correctly) form the complete objective formula.

exercise.instruction: Make teaching decisions and see how they affect student learning
exercise.scenario: You're planning a VR lesson about the water cycle for 8th graders. You have 30 minutes of VR time and 15 Meta Quest 3 headsets for 25 students.

n1.prompt: How will students experience the water cycle in VR?

a.label: Students manipulate water molecules through each stage
a.feedback: Active manipulation creates deep understanding. Students feel the energy changes and see cause-effect relationships.

b.label: Students watch a narrated 360° animation of the water cycle
b.feedback: Watching is passive. Students see the process but don't experience WHY it happens. This could be done with a video.

c.label: Students explore a virtual landscape and observe water cycle stages
c.feedback: Observation is better than narration, but students aren't causing the changes. They're still spectators.

n2.prompt: Since you have 15 headsets for 25 students, how will you manage the rotation?

a.label: First 15 use VR while others wait, then swap
a.feedback: Sitting and waiting for 15 minutes isn't great use of time. The waiting group disengages.

b.label: VR group does the activity while non-VR group completes a companion worksheet
b.feedback: Both groups are actively learning. The worksheet primes the non-VR group so they get more from their VR turn.

c.label: Skip VR for half the class — they can do it another day
c.feedback: This creates an uneven experience and wastes half the class period for half the students.

n3.endFeedback: Your choices shaped the learning experience. Active doing beats passive watching, and parallel activities keep everyone engaged. These principles apply to every VR lesson you design.

scenario.context: Your school purchased VR headsets. A teacher plans to use them exclusively for 360° virtual field trips — students will watch tours of museums, historical sites, and natural wonders.
scenario.question: What would you advise?

choices.a: Virtual field trips are the best use of VR — keep doing them
choices.b: 360° tours are a valid starting point, but they're passive observation. VR's real power is in active participation — add tasks like finding specific artefacts, making decisions, or solving problems within the environment.
choices.c: 360° tours are good enough — not every VR session needs to be interactive

feedback.a: Virtual field trips are engaging, but they're essentially watching. Students don't DO anything. VR's unique advantage — active practice with feedback — is completely unused. Adding interactive tasks would transform these from entertainment into learning.
feedback.b: Exactly. 360° tours are a starting point, not the destination. Adding a task — 'find three safety hazards,' 'identify the architectural style,' 'compare two exhibits' — turns passive watching into active learning. That's where VR's real impact lives.
feedback.c: For occasional enrichment, a 360° tour is fine. But if VR is a regular tool, passive observation alone doesn't justify the investment. Students retain ~10% of what they watch but up to 75% of what they practice. VR should tip toward active use.

c1.context: A biology teacher says: 'I use VR to let students walk through a virtual cell. They can look around and see all the organelles up close.'
c1.question: How could this be improved?

choices.a: Add a task: have students identify each organelle's function, diagnose a sick cell, or assemble a cell from its parts — turning observation into interaction.
choices.b: It's already perfect — exploring a cell in VR is inherently interactive.
choices.c: Replace VR with a microscope — real observation is always better.

feedback.a: Exactly. Walking through a cell is passive exploration. Adding a task — identify, diagnose, assemble — transforms it into active learning. The student goes from tourist to scientist.
feedback.b: Looking around is not interacting. True interaction means making choices, manipulating objects, or solving problems. Exploration without a task is virtual tourism.
feedback.c: Microscopes show real samples, but VR can show things impossible to see physically — like walking inside a cell. The issue isn't the tool, it's the lack of an active task.

c2.context: Two teachers are debating. Teacher A: 'My students watch a VR documentary about climate change.' Teacher B: 'My students make decisions as a city planner in a VR simulation, choosing policies and seeing their environmental impact over 50 years.'
c2.question: Which approach leverages VR better?

choices.a: Teacher A — documentaries convey more information
choices.b: Both are equally effective since both use VR headsets
choices.c: Teacher B — the simulation requires students to make decisions, see consequences, and learn through action, which is what VR does best

feedback.a: Documentaries can convey lots of information, but a VR documentary is still passive viewing. A flat screen would deliver the same content more comfortably. VR is justified when students DO something.
feedback.b: Using the same hardware doesn't mean the same learning outcome. Watching (Teacher A) is passive. Deciding and seeing consequences (Teacher B) is active. The activity design matters far more than the device.
feedback.c: Correct. Teacher B's simulation requires decision-making, consequence awareness, and iteration — all active learning. The student isn't watching climate change; they're causing and responding to it. That's VR's unique strength.

cls.instruction: Classify each VR activity as Seeing (passive) or Doing (active)
cls.i1: Watching a 360° guided tour of a factory
cls.i2: Operating virtual machinery following a step-by-step procedure
cls.i3: Making triage decisions on virtual patients in an emergency room
cls.i4: Viewing a fly-through of the human circulatory system
cls.i5: Assembling a virtual engine by selecting and placing parts correctly

exercise.instruction: Distribute the 40-minute VR class time across these phases
exercise.scenario: You have a 40-minute VR session with 25 students and 15 headsets. Allocate time for each phase to create a balanced lesson.

scenario.context: A teacher hands out VR headsets, says 'explore the virtual lab,' and collects them 20 minutes later. There's no briefing before and no discussion after.
scenario.question: What's wrong with this approach?

choices.a: The session is too long — 20 minutes causes eye strain
choices.b: Nothing wrong — letting students explore freely is the best approach
choices.c: Without briefing, students don't know what to focus on. Without debriefing, the experience stays as entertainment, not learning. A VR session needs all three phases: brief, experience, debrief.

feedback.a: Session length is a concern (15 min is better), but the bigger problem is the missing structure. Without a briefing to set the objective and a debrief to process the experience, even a perfectly timed session produces minimal learning.
feedback.b: Free exploration without a goal is virtual tourism. Students need a clear task (briefing), focused time to complete it (experience), and structured reflection (debriefing). Without this structure, VR is just an expensive toy.
feedback.c: Correct. The three phases are essential: Briefing gives purpose (what to do, what to look for). The experience is the active learning window. Debriefing transforms the experience into transferable knowledge. Skip any phase and learning suffers.

c1.context: During a VR session, you notice a student has stopped moving and seems confused. They've been standing still for 2 minutes.
c1.question: What should you do?

choices.a: Wait until the session ends — don't interrupt VR experiences
choices.b: Offer one brief, specific prompt to get them unstuck — 'Try looking to your left for the next clue' — then step back
choices.c: Take the headset off and show them what to do on the cast screen

feedback.a: Two minutes of confusion wastes learning time and can frustrate the student. The instructor's role during VR is to observe and provide a brief prompt when someone is clearly stuck.
feedback.b: Correct. A brief, targeted prompt preserves the student's autonomy while unblocking them. The goal is minimal intervention — just enough to get them moving again, not to solve the task for them.
feedback.c: Removing the headset breaks immersion entirely. A verbal prompt or gentle tap is less disruptive and keeps the student in the experience.

c2.context: After a VR session on emergency triage, you begin debriefing. A student says: 'It was cool.' Others nod and seem ready to move on.
c2.question: How do you respond?

choices.a: Use structured questions: 'Walk me through your first decision. What did you notice about the patient? What made you choose that priority level?' — dig into specific moments and choices.
choices.b: Accept it and move to the next activity — if students say it was cool, they learned something.
choices.c: Show the 'correct' answers and move on.

feedback.a: Correct. 'It was cool' means the experience was engaging, but learning hasn't been articulated yet. Structured questions force students to reconstruct their decisions, identify what worked, and connect the experience to real practice.
feedback.b: 'Cool' is an emotional response, not a learning outcome. Without reflection, students remember the feeling but not the lesson. Debriefing questions extract the learning from the experience.
feedback.c: Showing correct answers turns debriefing into a lecture. The goal is to help students discover insights by reflecting on their own actions — 'What did YOU decide and why?' — not to tell them the right answer.

s1.statement: A VR briefing should be under 60 seconds: state the objective, describe the task, set the time limit
s1.explanation: Long briefings kill momentum. Keep it short: what to do, how long, what success looks like. Students learn the rest by doing.

s2.statement: Debriefing is optional — the VR experience itself is enough for learning to occur
s2.explanation: Without debriefing, VR becomes entertainment. Students need to reflect on what they did, what worked, and how it connects to real practice. Debriefing is where the learning crystallises.

s3.statement: The instructor's role during VR is to observe via casting and only intervene when a student is clearly stuck
s3.explanation: The instructor monitors, notes strengths and errors for debriefing, and offers a brief prompt only when absolutely needed. The goal is minimal intervention to preserve student autonomy.

exercise.instruction: Match each learning goal to the most appropriate VR activity type

left.p1: Students need to familiarise themselves with a virtual hospital before a simulation
left.p2: Students must practice the fire evacuation procedure until they can do it correctly
left.p3: Students must demonstrate they can perform triage without any guidance
left.p4: Students are encountering a virtual archaeological site for the first time

scenario.context: You're planning a VR unit for nursing students. The topic is patient assessment. Students have never used VR before and have never seen the virtual hospital environment.
scenario.question: Which activity sequence would you use?

choices.a: Start with Guided Exploration (familiarise with the virtual hospital), then Simulation with Practice (perform patient assessments), then Evaluation (demonstrate competence without guidance)
choices.b: Go straight to Simulation with Practice — students learn by doing
choices.c: Only use Evaluation / Skill Check — the goal is to assess, not teach

feedback.a: Perfect sequence. Exploration first builds spatial familiarity so students aren't lost during the simulation. Practice develops the skill with feedback. Evaluation confirms competence. Each stage builds on the previous one.
feedback.b: Jumping to practice without exploration means students spend their first minutes figuring out where things are instead of focusing on the task. A short exploration phase (5 min) saves time in the simulation.
feedback.c: You can't evaluate a skill students haven't practiced. Evaluation comes after exploration and practice — it's the final checkpoint, not the starting point.

c1.context: A vocational instructor wants students to learn to operate a CNC milling machine. The students have studied the theory but have never seen one in operation.
c1.question: Which VR activity type should come first?

choices.a: Start with an Evaluation to see what they already know
choices.b: Start with Guided Exploration — let them walk around the virtual machine, observe its parts, and identify components before they operate it
choices.c: Skip VR — just use the real machine

feedback.a: Evaluating students who haven't practiced sets them up for failure. Start with exploration so they understand the environment, then practice operating the machine, then evaluate.
feedback.b: Correct. Guided Exploration lets students build mental models of the machine — where the controls are, what the parts do — without the pressure of operating it. This spatial familiarity pays off when they start the simulation.
feedback.c: Starting on real equipment without any practice is risky and expensive. VR exploration and simulation allow safe practice before students touch the real machine, building confidence and reducing errors.

c2.context: Students have completed three VR practice sessions on surgical hand hygiene. Their instructor wants to confirm they can perform the procedure independently before they enter the real operating room.
c2.question: Which VR activity type fits this goal?

choices.a: Another round of Guided Exploration
choices.b: More Simulation with Practice sessions
choices.c: Evaluation / Skill Check — students demonstrate the full procedure under assessment conditions, with no prompts or assistance

feedback.a: Exploration is for familiarisation with new environments. These students already know the space — they need to prove they can perform independently. That's assessment, not exploration.
feedback.b: More practice could help, but the instructor's goal is confirmation of competence. At some point, practice must transition to evaluation. Three sessions is sufficient preparation for a skill check.
feedback.c: Exactly. After sufficient practice, Evaluation / Skill Check verifies that students can perform independently under realistic conditions. This is the 'prove it' stage — no hints, no guidance, just performance.

cls.instruction: Classify each VR activity: is it Guided Exploration or Simulation with Practice?
cls.i1: Students walk through a virtual emergency department and identify potential safety hazards
cls.i2: Students practice administering an injection on a virtual patient with real-time feedback
cls.i3: Students perform a fire evacuation procedure, guiding virtual occupants to the correct exits
cls.i4: Students explore a virtual archaeological site and list the artefacts they find
cls.i5: Students assemble a virtual engine following the correct maintenance sequence

exercise.instruction: Arrange the classroom items into the correct zones for a safe VR session

scenario.context: Your school gives you a medium-sized classroom with large windows on one wall and rows of fixed desks. You need to run a VR session for 12 students next week.
scenario.question: What is the FIRST thing you should address in this room?

choices.a: Install blackout curtains on all the windows to prevent tracking issues
choices.b: Push desks to the walls to create open VR stations with clear floor space, then deal with lighting
choices.c: Request a bigger room — this one probably isn't suitable for VR

feedback.a: Curtains can help, but they're not the first priority. You can't run VR without clear floor space — students will trip on desks and bags. Address the physical layout first, then fine-tune lighting.
feedback.b: Exactly. The number one priority is clear, safe floor space. Desks, bags, and chairs become invisible hazards once headsets go on. Once the space is cleared and VR zones are marked, you can adjust blinds and lighting.
feedback.c: Most classrooms can work for VR with some rearrangement. Before requesting a new room, try clearing the existing space — you'll be surprised how well it works with just furniture pushed to the walls.

c1.context: During a test run, you notice the headset tracking jitters near the windows in bright afternoon sunlight.
c1.question: What should you do?

choices.a: Move all VR stations to the wall opposite the windows
choices.b: Close all the blinds and turn off the room lights entirely
choices.c: Ignore it — the headset will adjust automatically

feedback.a: Smart move. Repositioning VR stations away from direct sunlight fixes the tracking problem while keeping the room bright enough for supervision. You can also close the blinds on just the sunny side.
feedback.b: Closing blinds on the sunny side helps, but turning off all lights makes the room too dark to supervise safely. You need enough ambient light to see students, walk the room, and manage the session.
feedback.c: The headset won't adjust — inside-out tracking cameras are sensitive to direct sunlight and can produce jitter or lose tracking entirely. And worse, direct sunlight through the lenses can permanently damage the display.

c2.context: A colleague walks through your VR zone during setup and trips over a backpack a student left on the floor.
c2.question: What does this tell you about your room preparation?

choices.a: Backpacks should be stored at students' desks instead
choices.b: The VR zone needs to be clearly marked and the floor must be completely cleared of loose items before any session
choices.c: The colleague should have been more careful walking through the area

feedback.a: Storing backpacks at desks is one solution, but the real issue is that the VR zone wasn't cleared and marked. Any loose item on the floor is a tripping hazard — especially for students who can't see the floor while in VR.
feedback.b: Exactly. A VR zone must be visibly marked (tape, cones) and completely clear of obstacles. If an adult trips over a backpack, a student wearing a headset absolutely will too — and they can't see it coming.
feedback.c: If a sighted adult trips, a student wearing a VR headset has zero chance of avoiding the same obstacle. The issue is the room preparation, not the person walking through it.

cls.instruction: Which room features are essential for a VR session and which are nice-to-have?
cls.i1: Clear floor space (2x2m per station)
cls.i2: Carpet or foam mats on the floor
cls.i3: Instructor line of sight to all stations
cls.i5: Storage table near the exit for devices

exercise.instruction: Put the briefing steps in the correct order
exercise.i1: State the rules and safety expectations
exercise.i3: Explain the learning task and success criteria
exercise.i4: Set the time limit and rotation schedule

scenario.context: You're about to run your first VR session. Students are excited and already reaching for headsets on the table. A few are putting them on without waiting.
scenario.question: What should you do?

choices.a: Let the eager students start — their enthusiasm is valuable and shouldn't be dampened
choices.b: Have all students sit down, deliver the full briefing (rules, groups, roles, task), THEN distribute headsets
choices.c: Give a very quick 30-second overview and then hand out headsets to save time

feedback.a: Enthusiasm is great, but students who start without a briefing don't know the rules, the task, or their role. This leads to wasted VR time, safety issues, and students doing whatever they want instead of the learning activity.
feedback.b: Correct. No headsets until the briefing is complete. Students need to know the rules, their group, their role, the task, and the time limit before touching any device. Two minutes of structure prevents fifteen minutes of chaos.
feedback.c: A 30-second overview isn't enough. Students need to hear rules, know their group, understand their role, and know the specific task. Rushing the briefing means spending the entire VR session managing confusion.

c1.context: You assign roles: VR user, observer, facilitator. One student complains: 'I don't want to be an observer — I want to use VR.'
c1.question: How do you handle this?

choices.a: Explain that everyone rotates through all roles, and the observer's notes will be used in the debrief discussion — their contribution matters
choices.b: Let that student use VR first to avoid conflict and maintain class energy
choices.c: Remove the role system entirely since students don't like it

feedback.a: Perfect response. When students understand that every role rotates AND that each role contributes to the learning (observer notes feed the debrief), complaints drop significantly. The key is making every role feel purposeful.
feedback.b: Giving in sets a precedent that complaining gets you what you want. Other students will notice. The rotation system is fair — everyone gets equal VR time. Stand by it and explain why it matters.
feedback.c: Roles exist because they keep non-VR students engaged and learning. Without roles, students waiting for their turn have nothing to do, leading to boredom and behaviour problems. The system works — it just needs explanation.

c2.context: During the briefing, a student asks: 'What happens if I feel dizzy in VR?'
c2.question: What's the best response?

choices.a: Tell the student not to worry — dizziness is very rare and probably won't happen
choices.b: Explain clearly: remove the headset slowly, sit down, tell the instructor. It's normal, nothing to be embarrassed about, and you'll check on them.
choices.c: Say you'll deal with it if it happens — no need to worry about it now

feedback.a: Dismissing the concern doesn't prepare the student. Motion sickness does happen, especially with first-time users. If it occurs and the student wasn't told what to do, they may panic or suffer silently instead of asking for help.
feedback.b: Excellent. Clear, calm instructions before VR starts mean students know exactly what to do if they feel unwell. Normalizing it ('it's nothing to be embarrassed about') also prevents students from hiding discomfort to avoid looking weak.
feedback.c: Leaving it to chance means the student has no plan if it happens during VR. They might keep the headset on despite feeling sick, or panic. A 15-second explanation now prevents a problem later.

s1.statement: The briefing should happen BEFORE students touch the headsets
s1.explanation: Correct. Once headsets are on, students can't hear or see you effectively. All instructions, rules, group assignments, and task explanations must happen before any device is distributed.

s2.statement: Assigning roles (VR user, observer, facilitator) wastes valuable class time
s2.explanation: False. Roles keep ALL students engaged, not just those wearing headsets. Observers take notes for the debrief, facilitators assist with setup — every role contributes to the learning. Without roles, non-VR students have nothing to do.

s3.statement: Students should know the specific learning task before they put on the headset
s3.explanation: Correct. If students don't know what they're supposed to do in VR, they'll explore randomly instead of completing the learning activity. State the task and success criteria during the briefing.

exercise.instruction: Sort each preparation task by when it should be done
exercise.i1: Deep-clean all face pads and straps
exercise.i3: Verify the target app is installed and updated
exercise.i5: Test Boundary zones at each station
exercise.i6: Inventory controllers and check for damage
exercise.i7: Confirm WiFi credentials on each device
exercise.i8: Line up numbered headsets on the storage table

scenario.context: You arrive at school 30 minutes before your VR class. You open the storage cabinet and discover that 2 of your 4 headsets didn't charge overnight — they're at 15% battery.
scenario.question: What do you do?

choices.a: Cancel the VR activity and run a backup lesson instead
choices.b: Plug in the 2 dead headsets, start with the 2 working headsets and adjust rotation groups. Add the others when they have enough charge.
choices.c: Run the session anyway — 15% should last long enough for one rotation

feedback.a: Cancelling is an overreaction. You still have 2 working headsets, which is enough to run a rotation with adjusted groups. Adaptability is a core VR teaching skill — work with what you have.
feedback.b: Perfect. Start the session with your 2 charged headsets and plug in the dead ones. Adjust from 3 rotation groups to 2 temporarily. When the charging headsets hit 50–60%, add them to the rotation. No VR time is lost.
feedback.c: 15% battery won't last a full rotation. A Quest 3 at 15% might die mid-session, disrupting the student's experience and wasting their VR time. Plug them in and use the working ones first.

c1.context: Students keep mixing up controllers between headsets. Controllers paired to the wrong headset cause connection issues and delays.
c1.question: What's the best prevention strategy?

choices.a: Label each headset and its controllers with matching numbers or coloured tape, and store each set in its own labelled container
choices.b: Let students figure it out — they'll learn which controller goes with which headset
choices.c: Only use one headset at a time so there's no confusion

feedback.a: This is the standard best practice. Numbered or colour-coded sets (headset #1 = red tape, controllers #1 = red tape) take minutes to set up and eliminate pairing confusion permanently. Storing them together prevents mixing.
feedback.b: Students don't know which controller pairs with which headset — the pairing is invisible. They'll grab whatever is closest, causing connection failures and wasting 5–10 minutes per session. Labels solve this instantly.
feedback.c: Using one headset at a time means 24 of 25 students sit idle. The whole point of having multiple headsets is to maximise VR time. Labels and organisation let you run all headsets simultaneously.

c2.context: You have 4 headsets ready on the table. 12 students are seated after the briefing. It's time to distribute.
c2.question: What's the most efficient distribution method?

choices.a: Let students come up to the table and grab a headset
choices.b: Have students raise hands and distribute headsets randomly
choices.c: Call up the first rotation group by name, hand each student their numbered headset, confirm the fit, then begin

feedback.a: Letting students grab headsets creates a rush to the table, arguments over who gets which device, and chaos. Control the distribution — it takes 60 seconds and prevents 5 minutes of disorder.
feedback.b: Random distribution ignores your planned groups and makes tracking harder. If headset #3 goes to a random student, you can't find it later if there's a problem. Follow the group plan.
feedback.c: Exactly right. Calling groups by name maintains order. Handing each student their numbered headset keeps pairing correct. Confirming fit (straps, IPD) before saying 'go' prevents mid-session interruptions.

s1.statement: You should install and test every app on every headset the day BEFORE class, not during class
s1.explanation: Correct. App downloads and updates can take 10–30 minutes and require stable WiFi. Discovering a missing app during class wastes everyone's VR time. Test everything the night before.

s2.statement: If a headset is at 50% battery, it's fine for a full 50-minute class
s2.explanation: False. A Quest 3 at 50% has roughly 1 hour of active use. With overhead from casting, WiFi, and multiple rotations, 50% may not last the full session. Always start at 80% or higher.

s3.statement: Numbering headsets and matching controllers with coloured labels prevents pairing problems during class
s3.explanation: Correct. Colour-coded or numbered sets eliminate the #1 time waster in VR classrooms: students grabbing the wrong controllers. A sticker on each device takes 2 minutes to set up and saves hundreds of minutes over the year.

exercise.instruction: Navigate the decisions you face during a live VR session
exercise.scenario: You're 5 minutes into a VR rotation. Four students are in headsets, four are observing the cast screen, four are at the complementary station.

n1.prompt: You notice a VR student has stopped doing the assigned task and is wandering around the virtual environment exploring freely.

a.label: Walk over and quietly redirect: 'Remember, your task is to find the three safety hazards. You have 6 minutes left.'
a.feedback: Good choice. A calm, specific verbal redirect keeps the student on task without startling them or disrupting others. Naming the task and the time left gives them clear direction.

b.label: Immediately remove the headset — they're wasting VR time
b.feedback: Removing a headset abruptly is startling and embarrassing. It should only be used for safety emergencies. For off-task behaviour, a verbal redirect is more effective and respectful.

c.label: Ignore it — they'll get back on task eventually
c.feedback: They probably won't. Without redirection, the student will spend the entire rotation exploring and miss the learning activity. A brief verbal cue takes 5 seconds and saves the whole rotation.

n2.prompt: An observer raises her hand: 'The casting screen just went black. We can't see what the VR students are doing.'

a.label: Tell observers to just wait — you'll fix the casting after the rotation ends
a.feedback: Leaving observers with nothing to see for the rest of the rotation wastes their learning time. Casting is their window into the VR experience. A quick fix now keeps everyone productive.

b.label: Ask the observer to check the casting device's WiFi connection while you continue circulating
b.feedback: Great delegation. Having an observer troubleshoot the casting (check WiFi, restart the cast) empowers students and keeps you free to monitor VR students. If it doesn't work, you can step in after your round.

c.label: Stop the entire session so everyone can troubleshoot the casting together
c.feedback: Stopping VR for a casting issue disrupts students mid-task. Casting loss is inconvenient but not a safety issue. Keep VR students going while resolving the casting separately.

n3.endFeedback: Effective session management means redirecting gently, delegating small fixes, and keeping the session moving. You don't need to solve every problem yourself — and you should never pull a student out of VR unless safety is at risk.

scenario.context: During a live VR session, a student in VR calls out: 'This is boring, I already finished.' Meanwhile, you notice another VR student has stepped outside their taped boundary and is getting close to a desk.
scenario.question: What's your priority?

choices.a: Address the student who finished early — boredom leads to misbehaviour
choices.b: Address the student outside their boundary — physical safety is always the top priority
choices.c: Pause the entire session and re-explain the boundary rules to everyone

feedback.a: Boredom is a pedagogical concern, but the student outside their boundary is a physical safety risk. They can't see the desk they're about to hit. Safety always comes before pedagogy — redirect the boundary student first, then give the early finisher a new task.
feedback.b: Exactly right. A student outside their marked zone while wearing a headset is a collision risk — they can't see obstacles. Walk to them calmly, use their name, and guide them back. The early finisher can wait 30 seconds; the boundary student cannot.
feedback.c: Stopping everything penalises the students who are on task and in their zones. Address the boundary issue individually, then the early finisher. A full stop is only needed for genuine whole-class safety problems.

c1.context: You have 4 headsets active but your casting setup can only display one headset's view at a time on the projector.
c1.question: Which student should you cast?

choices.a: Cast the student who's struggling most — you can coach them from outside
choices.b: Rotate the cast every 3–4 minutes across different students so observers see varied approaches
choices.c: Cast the strongest student to model the correct way to complete the task

feedback.a: Coaching a struggling student through casting can work, but observers learn more from seeing varied approaches. If only one student is cast the whole time, observers see one perspective. Rotating keeps them engaged and shows different strategies.
feedback.b: Rotating the cast gives observers multiple perspectives — they see different strategies, different mistakes, and different pacing. This creates richer material for the debriefing discussion and keeps observers actively watching instead of zoning out.
feedback.c: Showing only the best student means observers see one 'right' way. They miss the learning that comes from seeing mistakes, different strategies, and varied pacing. Variety is more educational than perfection.

c2.context: While circulating the room during a VR session, you notice that one student keeps restarting the scenario from the beginning instead of progressing forward.
c2.question: What should you note for the debrief?

choices.a: Note it as off-task behaviour to address privately after class
choices.b: Note the specific point where they restart — this likely indicates a confusing instruction or interface element. Discuss in the debrief to help the whole class.
choices.c: Don't note anything — it's the student's problem to solve on their own

feedback.a: Restarting isn't necessarily off-task — the student may be confused about what to do next. Treating it as misbehaviour misses the opportunity to identify and fix a common confusion point that other students might share.
feedback.b: Exactly. Repeated restarts usually signal a misunderstanding — unclear instructions, a confusing interface, or a step the student can't figure out. Noting the exact restart point lets you address it in the debrief, helping everyone who might hit the same wall.
feedback.c: Observation notes are one of the instructor's most important tools. If a student is stuck, that's information you need for the debrief. Ignoring it means losing a teaching opportunity.

cls.instruction: Classify each responsibility as belonging to the student or the instructor during a live VR session
cls.i1: Stay inside the taped boundary zone
cls.i2: Cast at least one headset to the shared screen
cls.i3: Keep wrist straps on at all times
cls.i4: Take observation notes on learning behaviours
cls.i5: Raise a hand if feeling dizzy or unwell

exercise.instruction: Distribute 50 minutes across the phases of a VR class with 4 headsets and 12 students (3 groups)
exercise.scenario: You have a 50-minute class, 4 VR headsets, and 12 students. Plan time for briefing, 3 rotations, transitions between them, and a wrap-up.

scenario.context: You have 6 VR headsets but 24 students in class. The period is 50 minutes. A colleague suggests running 4 groups of 6 students, with each group getting 12 minutes of VR time.
scenario.question: What is the main problem with this approach?

choices.a: 12 minutes of VR per rotation is too long — students will lose focus
choices.b: 4 rotations x 12 min = 48 min — with no time left for briefing, transitions, or wrap-up. You need to plan the full 50 minutes, not just VR time.
choices.c: 6 headsets at once is too many for one teacher to supervise safely

feedback.a: 12 minutes is actually within the recommended range of 10–15 minutes. The problem isn't duration — it's that the math ignores all the non-VR time. Briefing, transitions, and wrap-up need minutes too.
feedback.b: Exactly. Beginners plan VR time and forget everything else. A realistic plan: 4 min briefing + 4 x 10 min rotations + 3 x 2 min transitions = 50 min exactly. Or reduce to 3 rotations with 2 groups doubled up. Always plan the full period.
feedback.c: 6 headsets is manageable for an experienced teacher, especially with the casting screen. The real issue is the math — there's no room for briefing or wrap-up in the plan.

c1.context: During a 3-station rotation (VR / observation / complementary), students at the complementary station finish their worksheet in 3 minutes and have nothing to do for the remaining 7 minutes.
c1.question: How do you fix this?

choices.a: Design a more substantial complementary activity: research task, discussion questions, or preparation for their VR turn
choices.b: Shorten the rotation so the complementary station finishes at the same time as the others
choices.c: Eliminate the complementary station — students can just wait for their VR turn

feedback.a: Perfect. The complementary station should be as engaging as the VR station. A research task ('Find three examples of how this concept applies in the real world'), discussion cards, or preparing observation questions for the next rotation keeps students productive the entire time.
feedback.b: Shortening the rotation penalises VR and observation students who need the full time. The fix is a better complementary activity, not less time for everyone else.
feedback.c: Students with nothing to do for 10 minutes will become disruptive. Every station needs a purposeful activity. The complementary station isn't filler — it's where students process and prepare for their VR turn.

c2.context: It's your students' very first VR session ever. You planned 3 rotations of 10 minutes each with a complex simulation app.
c2.question: Is this a good plan for a first session?

choices.a: It's too aggressive — plan extra time for headset fitting, Boundary tutorial, and orientation. Use a simple app and consider only 2 rotations.
choices.b: It's fine — students will figure it out as they go, and excitement will carry them through
choices.c: Add more rotations so every student gets extra VR time on their first day

feedback.a: Correct. First sessions need extra time for everything: adjusting straps, explaining controllers, setting up Boundary zones, and managing excitement. Use the simplest possible app (a 360° tour, not a simulation) and expect to lose time to logistics. Two 8-minute rotations is more realistic.
feedback.b: Excitement creates chaos, not learning. First-timers need time to adjust the headset, learn the controllers, and feel comfortable. Jumping into a complex simulation without this foundation means students spend VR time confused instead of learning.
feedback.c: More rotations on day one means more transitions, more headset-fitting delays, and more overwhelm. Keep it simple: shorter rotations, simple app, focus on comfort. Save the complex activities for session two.

s1.statement: A 3-station rotation (VR, observation, complementary) keeps all students engaged even with limited headsets
s1.explanation: Correct. Station rotation means every student has a purposeful task at all times — not just the ones wearing headsets. Observers learn from the cast, complementary station students prepare or reflect. Nobody sits idle.

s2.statement: Transitions between rotations don't need to be planned — students will switch quickly on their own
s2.explanation: False. Unplanned transitions waste 3–5 minutes per switch as students argue, fumble with headsets, and lose focus. Plan 2-minute transitions with clear signals (timer, bell) and assigned roles for headset handoff.

s3.statement: The shutdown routine (headsets off, wipe, charge) should be built into the 50-minute plan, not treated as an afterthought
s3.explanation: Correct. If you don't plan 3–5 minutes for shutdown, class ends with headsets scattered, face pads unsanitized, and batteries draining overnight. A consistent routine protects your equipment and sets up the next session.

exercise.instruction: Match each VR activity type or safety feature to its correct description

p4.def: Activate when the user steps outside the play area

scenario.context: Your school's science lab has a 4×6m open area in the centre, but desks line the walls and there are backpacks on the floor. You need to run a room-scale VR activity tomorrow.
scenario.question: What is your first priority?

choices.a: Calculate how many students can use VR at once based on the 3×3m per station requirement
choices.b: Clear all obstacles from the floor, mark boundaries with tape, then determine how many stations fit safely
choices.c: Switch to a seated experience instead — the room is too small for room-scale

feedback.a: Calculating stations is important, but the first priority is clearing the physical space. Backpacks and loose items are invisible hazards for students wearing headsets — clear first, then measure.
feedback.b: Exactly. The number one step is clearing the physical space of obstacles. Backpacks, cables, and loose items are tripping hazards for VR users who can't see the floor. Once the space is clear and marked, you can determine station count.
feedback.c: A 4×6m area actually fits one 3×3m room-scale station comfortably. Don't default to less immersive experiences when the space works — just clear and mark it properly.

c1.context: A student in VR is walking toward the wall. The Boundary grid appears, but the student ignores it and keeps moving.
c1.question: What should have been set up differently?

choices.a: The Boundary should be set tighter so the grid appears earlier, giving the student more warning distance from walls
choices.b: The Boundary alone is not enough — the instructor should be watching and give a verbal warning before the student reaches any wall
choices.c: The room should have foam padding on all walls to prevent injuries

feedback.a: Good thinking. Setting the Boundary a comfortable distance from walls gives students more reaction time. Combined with instructor supervision, this creates a double layer of safety.
feedback.b: Relying on instructor reaction time alone is risky. The Boundary system provides an automatic visual warning before the student reaches the edge. Setting it tighter gives more buffer. The best approach combines both: tighter Boundary + active supervision.
feedback.c: Wall padding addresses the symptom, not the cause. The real fix is proper Boundary configuration and instructor supervision to prevent students from reaching walls at all.

c2.context: You only have a 3×2 metre area available for VR in your classroom.
c2.question: Which experience type should you choose?

choices.a: Room-scale — students can still move if they're careful
choices.b: Standing or seated — both fit safely within this space
choices.c: Cancel VR — this space is too small for any VR activity

feedback.a: Room-scale requires 3×3m minimum. A 3×2m area doesn't meet this requirement, and 'being careful' is not a safety strategy. Students in VR can't see the real world — they need sufficient space.
feedback.b: Correct. Standing VR needs 2×2m and seated needs 1×1m — both fit within 3×2m. Matching the experience to the available space is a key safety decision.
feedback.c: A 3×2m area is plenty for standing (2×2m) and seated (1×1m) VR experiences. Don't cancel VR when the space works for other activity types.

cls.instruction: Which space category does each VR setup belong to?
cls.i1: Controllers only, user stays in a chair
cls.i2: Walking, crouching, and reaching freely
cls.i3: Turning and leaning in place, no walking

exercise.instruction: Label each statement as Fact or Myth about VR supervision

s1.statement: It's okay to step out briefly if students are experienced VR users
s1.explanation: Myth. Never leave VR users unsupervised, regardless of experience level. Accidents happen without warning — an experienced user can still trip, feel dizzy, or bump into someone.

s2.statement: You should configure the Boundary system on every headset before each session
s2.explanation: Fact. The Boundary system is a critical safety layer. It must be set up on every headset before any student puts it on, every session.

s3.statement: An instructor can safely supervise up to 30 students in VR simultaneously
s3.explanation: Myth. You can only safely supervise the number of VR users you can maintain direct line of sight to. With 30 students, only a small group should be in VR while others observe or do companion tasks.

s4.statement: Students not in VR can observe via screen casting while waiting for their turn
s4.explanation: Fact. Screen casting lets non-VR students see what VR users are experiencing. This keeps them engaged, supports observation-based learning, and reduces idle waiting time.

s5.statement: The Boundary system makes instructor supervision unnecessary
s5.explanation: Myth. The Boundary is an automated warning, not a substitute for human supervision. It cannot detect obstacles that move (like other students), respond to dizziness, or manage session logistics.

scenario.context: You have 20 students and 5 VR headsets. A colleague suggests all 5 students can be in VR simultaneously while you supervise from your desk at the front.
scenario.question: What is your main concern?

choices.a: Five students at once is fine — the Boundary system will keep them safe
choices.b: Only if you can maintain direct line of sight to all 5 stations from where you're positioned — rearrange if needed
choices.c: You should only have 1 student in VR at a time to be completely safe

feedback.a: The Boundary system is helpful but doesn't replace supervision. It can't detect moving obstacles, respond to students feeling unwell, or manage session logistics. You still need line of sight.
feedback.b: Correct. The key question is whether you can see all active VR users from your position. If your desk doesn't give you line of sight to every station, move to a central position or reduce the number of simultaneous users.
feedback.c: One at a time is extremely safe but impractical — with 20 students and 50 minutes, each would get only 2.5 minutes of VR. Supervising 3–5 users simultaneously is realistic if you have clear sightlines.

c1.context: During a VR session, a colleague calls you from the hallway asking for a quick word. Three students are currently wearing headsets.
c1.question: What do you do?

choices.a: Stay in the room — you cannot leave VR users unsupervised, even briefly. Ask the colleague to wait or come to you.
choices.b: Step out quickly — it will only take a minute and the students are in the middle of their activity
choices.c: Ask a student who isn't in VR to watch the three VR users while you step out

feedback.a: Correct. The rule is absolute: never leave VR users unsupervised. If the colleague needs you, they can step into the room or wait until the session is over.
feedback.b: Even 'just a minute' is too long. In that time, a student could walk into a wall, feel dizzy and fall, or collide with another student. If you must leave, headsets come off first.
feedback.c: An untrained student is not a substitute for instructor supervision. They don't know how to handle Boundary alerts, dizziness, or collisions. Stay in the room.

c2.context: You notice a student has turned off the Boundary because they find the grid overlay distracting during their VR experience.
c2.question: What do you do?

choices.a: Let them continue — if the grid is distracting, it could interfere with the learning experience
choices.b: Increase the room lighting so you can supervise better without the Boundary
choices.c: Turn the Boundary back on immediately — it's a non-negotiable safety feature, even if it's briefly visible during the experience

feedback.a: The Boundary is not optional. Without it, the student has no warning before walking into walls, furniture, or other people. A brief visual grid is a minor inconvenience compared to a collision.
feedback.b: Room lighting helps you see the students, but it doesn't help the student inside VR see the real world. Only the Boundary system provides that warning.
feedback.c: Correct. The Boundary system is non-negotiable. It's the student's only automated warning that they're approaching a physical boundary. Explain why it matters and adjust the sensitivity if the grid appears too often.

s1.statement: The instructor must maintain direct line of sight to all VR users throughout the entire session
s1.explanation: True. This is the most important supervision rule. If you cannot see a VR user, you cannot respond to safety issues.

s2.statement: Experienced VR users can be left unsupervised for short periods
s2.explanation: False. Experience does not prevent dizziness, tripping, or unexpected incidents. The no-unsupervised-VR rule applies to all users, always.

s3.statement: If you are the only instructor, you should limit the number of students in VR at the same time
s3.explanation: True. One instructor can only safely monitor a limited number of active VR users. The remaining students should observe via casting, do companion tasks, or wait for their rotation.

exercise.instruction: Arrange the five steps of the disorientation response protocol in the correct order
exercise.i1: Ask the student to stop all movement
exercise.i4: Allow recovery time — let them rest quietly
exercise.i5: Do not rush them back to VR this session

scenario.context: A student removes their headset mid-session and says 'I feel weird.' Their face looks pale and they're unsteady on their feet.
scenario.question: What do you do first?

choices.a: Tell them to sit back down and try again in 5 minutes — it usually passes quickly
choices.b: Guide them to sit down immediately, offer water, let them rest, and do not pressure them to return to VR this session
choices.c: Send them to the school nurse immediately — they might have a serious condition

feedback.a: Never tell a student experiencing VR discomfort to 'try again.' Pushing through makes symptoms worse, not better. The student needs to sit, rest, and skip VR for the remainder of the session.
feedback.b: Correct. The priority is getting them seated safely and letting them recover. Disorientation typically passes within minutes. No water, rest, calm voice — and absolutely no pressure to go back into VR during this session.
feedback.c: Most VR disorientation is mild and resolves in minutes with rest. Sending them to the nurse immediately is an overreaction unless symptoms persist beyond 10 minutes or include vomiting or severe headache.

c1.context: You're choosing between two VR apps for a class session. App A involves flying through a city at high speed. App B lets students walk at their own pace through a virtual museum.
c1.question: Which is better for preventing motion sickness?

choices.a: App A — the excitement will distract students from any discomfort
choices.b: App B — self-paced movement with no forced locomotion is much less likely to cause cybersickness
choices.c: Neither — motion sickness in VR is purely random and can't be prevented by app choice

feedback.a: Excitement doesn't prevent cybersickness — forced, fast movement is one of its primary triggers. Flying simulations and roller coasters are among the worst offenders for motion sickness.
feedback.b: Correct. Self-paced movement where the student controls their own locomotion is far less likely to cause cybersickness. Avoid apps with forced movement (flying, roller coasters, fast vehicles) especially for beginners.
feedback.c: App choice matters significantly. Forced high-speed movement is a known trigger for cybersickness. Self-paced, walking-speed experiences with user-controlled locomotion dramatically reduce the risk.

c2.context: A student finished their VR session 5 minutes ago but still feels dizzy and slightly nauseous.
c2.question: What do you do?

choices.a: Let them continue resting — most symptoms resolve within a few more minutes. If it persists past 10 minutes, they should skip VR for the rest of the day.
choices.b: Put them back in VR with a calmer app — sometimes re-exposure helps the brain adjust
choices.c: Ignore it — they probably just stood up too fast after sitting in VR

feedback.a: Correct. Five minutes of lingering symptoms is within normal range. Let them rest, offer water, and monitor. If symptoms don't improve after 10 minutes total, VR is done for the day. Never rush recovery.
feedback.b: Never re-expose a symptomatic student to VR. Re-entering VR while still dizzy will make symptoms significantly worse. The student needs full recovery before any consideration of returning — and not in this session.
feedback.c: Dismissing a student's symptoms is unsafe and undermines trust. Dizziness after VR is a recognised phenomenon (cybersickness) that requires rest, not dismissal.

cls.instruction: Is this a prevention strategy or a response action?
cls.i1: Keep VR sessions to 5–15 minutes
cls.i2: Help remove the headset gently
cls.i3: Avoid apps with forced high-speed movement
cls.i4: Guide the student to sit down and rest
cls.i5: Offer seated mode when the app supports it

exercise.instruction: Sort each cleaning task into the correct category
exercise.i1: Wipe face pad with non-alcoholic disinfectant wipe
exercise.i2: Remove and wash detachable face pad thoroughly
exercise.i5: Inspect lenses and clean with microfibre cloth (circular motions)
exercise.i6: Use alcohol-based cleaner on foam face pad
exercise.i7: Allow 30 seconds of air drying before next user
exercise.i8: Store headsets in a clean, dry location with lenses away from light

scenario.context: Between rotations, a student hands you a headset with visible sweat on the face pad. The next student is waiting.
scenario.question: What do you do?

choices.a: Wipe it quickly with your sleeve and hand it over — speed is important during rotations
choices.b: Use a non-alcoholic disinfectant wipe on the face pad and controller grips, let it air dry briefly, then hand it to the next student
choices.c: Skip this headset and use a different one instead

feedback.a: Clothing can harbour bacteria and doesn't disinfect. Using your sleeve is unhygienic for the next user and doesn't address the sweat properly. Always use proper cleaning supplies.
feedback.b: Correct. A 60-second wipe-down with proper supplies is all it takes. Non-alcoholic disinfectant for the face pad and controller grips, brief air dry, and the headset is ready. This protects both equipment and students.
feedback.c: Having a spare is nice, but avoidance doesn't solve the problem — the sweaty headset still needs cleaning before anyone else uses it. Build the cleaning step into every rotation handoff.

c1.context: A colleague suggests using paper towels to quickly clean fingerprints off the VR headset lenses between users.
c1.question: Why is this a problem?

choices.a: Paper towels are too slow — there are faster methods
choices.b: Paper towels are abrasive and can scratch the special coatings on VR lenses. Only a dry microfibre cloth should be used, with gentle circular motions.
choices.c: Paper towels leave too much moisture on the lenses

feedback.a: Speed isn't the issue — lens damage is. Paper towels have wood fibres that scratch the delicate anti-reflective and anti-smudge coatings on VR lenses.
feedback.b: Correct. VR lenses have specialised coatings that are easily damaged by abrasive materials. Paper towels, tissues, and clothing can all cause micro-scratches. Only a dry microfibre cloth should touch the lenses.
feedback.c: Moisture isn't the main concern — physical damage is. Paper towel fibres are abrasive enough to scratch lens coatings permanently. The correct tool is always a dry microfibre cloth.

c2.context: At the end of the day, you need to store 6 VR headsets until next week's session.
c2.question: What's the correct procedure?

choices.a: Stack them in a box and put it in the storage cupboard
choices.b: Do a full deep clean (face pads, shell, controllers, lenses), then store each headset in a clean, dry location with lenses facing away from any light source
choices.c: Leave them on the table plugged in to charge — they'll be ready for next week

feedback.a: Stacking can damage lenses and straps. Skipping the deep clean means sweat and bacteria sit on the equipment for a week. Both reduce equipment lifespan and hygiene.
feedback.b: Correct. The end-of-day routine is: clean everything thoroughly, inspect lenses, and store properly. Lenses facing away from light prevents sun damage through windows. A clean, dry location prevents mould on foam padding.
feedback.c: Leaving headsets on a table risks accidental damage and sun exposure to lenses. Charging them is good, but they also need a deep clean and proper storage.

s1.statement: Alcohol-based cleaners are the best choice for VR headset face pads
s1.explanation: False. Alcohol damages foam materials and can cause skin irritation. Use non-alcoholic disinfectant wipes specifically suited for VR equipment.

s2.statement: The between-student cleaning routine should take about 60 seconds
s2.explanation: True. A quick wipe of the face pad, controller grips, and a brief air dry is all that's needed between users. It's fast enough to fit into rotation transitions.

s3.statement: VR headset lenses should only be cleaned with a dry microfibre cloth using gentle circular motions
s3.explanation: True. VR lenses have delicate coatings that are easily scratched by paper towels, tissues, or clothing. Only microfibre cloth preserves the lens quality.

exercise.instruction: Navigate this scenario: a student tells you they don't want to use VR today
exercise.scenario: A quiet student approaches you before the VR session and says they'd rather not put on a headset today. They don't give a reason.

n1.prompt: The student has told you they don't want to use VR. What do you do?

a.label: Respect their decision and immediately offer an alternative role
a.feedback: Excellent. Participation is always voluntary. By respecting the boundary without questioning it and offering an alternative, you maintain trust and keep the student engaged in learning.

b.label: Ask them to try it for just 2 minutes to see if they like it
b.feedback: Pressuring a student — even gently — after they've communicated a boundary undermines trust. If they said no, respect it. There may be a reason they don't want to share.

c.label: Tell them it's part of the grade and they need to participate
c.feedback: VR participation should never be mandatory. Forcing students into VR can create anxiety, worsen existing conditions, or damage trust. Always have a non-VR path to the same learning objective.

n2.prompt: You've offered the student an active observer role. What task do you give them?

a.label: Tell them to just watch the casting screen quietly
a.feedback: Watching without structure is passive — the student will disengage quickly. Observation without a task is not active learning; it's just sitting out.

b.label: Give them a checklist: note what the VR user does at each step, identify one mistake, and prepare feedback for the debrief
b.feedback: Perfect. A structured observation task makes the observer role genuinely educational. The student engages with the content, develops critical thinking, and contributes to the class discussion.

c.label: Ask them to take photos of the casting screen for the class journal
c.feedback: This gives them a task, but it's logistical rather than educational. The student captures images but doesn't engage with the learning content. A structured observation checklist would be more valuable.

n3.prompt: The observer completes their structured task and is ready for the debriefing.
n3.endFeedback: Well handled. The student participated meaningfully without wearing a headset: they observed, analysed, and prepared feedback. During the debrief, their perspective as an outside observer often adds insights the VR users missed. Not wearing a headset does not mean not learning.

scenario.context: Three students in your class cannot use VR today — one has a headache, one is prone to cybersickness, and one simply doesn't want to. All three are sitting out.
scenario.question: How do you handle this?

choices.a: Excuse all three from the activity and let them do independent study at their desks
choices.b: Respect each student's situation, assign them meaningful alternative roles (observer, discussion leader, analyst), and ensure they participate in the debrief
choices.c: Try to convince the reluctant student — the other two have valid reasons, but this student should at least try

feedback.a: Independent study is exclusion dressed up as flexibility. The students miss the VR-related learning entirely. Alternative roles like observer, analyst, or discussion leader keep them connected to the same learning objectives.
feedback.b: Correct. Every student's reason is valid — you don't need to rank them. Assign meaningful roles that engage with the same content: active observer, note-taker for the debrief, or discussion leader. All three should contribute during the debrief alongside VR users.
feedback.c: You don't get to decide which reasons for declining VR are 'valid enough.' Participation is always voluntary, no exceptions. Respect all three boundaries equally and offer the same quality of alternative.

c1.context: A parent emails saying their child has epilepsy and should not use VR under any circumstances. The next VR session is in two days.
c1.question: How do you respond?

choices.a: Acknowledge the concern, confirm the student will not use VR, and explain the meaningful alternative role they'll have during VR sessions
choices.b: Ask for a doctor's note to verify the condition before making accommodations
choices.c: Suggest the student stay home on VR days to avoid any issues

feedback.a: Correct. Acknowledge, accommodate, and communicate the plan. The parent needs to know their child will still participate in meaningful learning — just through a different pathway. No medical verification is needed; you trust the parent.
feedback.b: Requiring medical proof creates unnecessary barriers and signals distrust. The parent has informed you of a medical condition — your job is to accommodate, not verify.
feedback.c: Excluding a student from school because of a medical condition is discriminatory. VR is a tool, not the lesson itself. The student can achieve the same learning objective through alternative roles.

c2.context: After a VR session, an observer student says: 'I didn't learn anything because I didn't get to use VR. Everyone who wore a headset had a much better experience.'
c2.question: How do you respond?

choices.a: Promise them they'll definitely get a VR turn next time
choices.b: Ask what they observed, what they noticed the VR user did well or poorly, and highlight how their outside perspective gives them insights the VR users didn't have
choices.c: Acknowledge they missed out and offer to let them stay after class for a quick VR session

feedback.a: Promising a future turn doesn't address the current feeling and implies VR is the 'real' experience while observation is lesser. Instead, validate the observer role itself.
feedback.b: Correct. Reframe the observer experience as a strength, not a deficit. Ask them to share specific observations: what did they see that VR users missed? Their outside perspective often catches things immersion hides. This validates the role and builds their confidence.
feedback.c: This well-meaning response reinforces the idea that the observer role is a consolation prize. Instead, validate observation as a genuine learning experience with unique advantages.

cls.instruction: Is this activity typically done by the VR user or the active observer?
cls.i1: Performs the simulation inside the headset
cls.i2: Predicts outcomes before the VR user acts
cls.i3: Takes structured notes via the casting screen
cls.i4: Interacts with virtual objects using controllers
cls.i5: Identifies mistakes the VR user doesn't notice

exercise.instruction: Put the four components of the O-R-T-T briefing framework in the correct order
exercise.i1: Objective — state what the learner will accomplish
exercise.i2: Rules — explain boundaries, safety, what is allowed
exercise.i3: Time — tell them how long the experience will last
exercise.i4: Task — describe the specific actions to perform

scenario.context: You're about to run a VR safety hazard identification session with 15 students. Most have never used VR before. You have 2 minutes before headsets go on.
scenario.question: How do you prepare the students?

choices.a: Hand out the headsets and let students explore freely — they'll figure it out
choices.b: Give a structured 60-second briefing: state the objective, explain the rules and task, set the time limit, then hand out headsets
choices.c: Spend 10 minutes explaining every button on the controller, all the menus, and how the Boundary system works technically

feedback.a: Without a briefing, students don't know what to focus on. They'll spend VR time figuring out controls instead of learning. First-time users especially need clear expectations before entering VR.
feedback.b: Exactly right. A structured briefing using the O-R-T-T framework (Objective, Rules, Time, Task) gives students everything they need to focus on learning, not guessing. Keep it under 60 seconds.
feedback.c: This is over-briefing. A 10-minute technical explanation overwhelms students and steals time from the actual VR experience. Limit hardware orientation to 30 seconds — the rest is about the learning objective.

c1.context: A student looks visibly nervous before putting on a VR headset for the first time. They keep asking what will happen inside.
c1.question: What is the most effective response?

choices.a: Reassure them by briefly stating the objective, what they'll do, how long it lasts, and that they can stop at any time — this is what briefing does
choices.b: Show them a video of someone else using VR first so they can see what it looks like
choices.c: Tell them not to worry — VR is fun and they'll love it once they try

feedback.a: Perfect. One of the key benefits of briefing is anxiety reduction. When learners know what to expect — the goal, the task, the duration, and their ability to stop — uncertainty drops and they can focus on learning.
feedback.b: A video can help, but it takes extra time and doesn't address the student's specific concerns. A direct, structured briefing that covers what they'll do, how long it lasts, and their option to stop is faster and more effective.
feedback.c: Dismissing anxiety with 'you'll love it' doesn't help. The student needs information, not reassurance. A briefing that answers their questions directly (what will happen, how long, can I stop?) reduces anxiety far more effectively.

c2.context: During a VR session, several students seem confused — they're wandering aimlessly inside the virtual environment and asking each other what they're supposed to do.
c2.question: What most likely went wrong?

choices.a: The VR app is poorly designed and doesn't give clear instructions
choices.b: The briefing was missing or incomplete — students didn't receive a clear objective or task description before entering VR
choices.c: The students weren't paying attention during the briefing

feedback.a: While app design matters, a well-briefed student knows exactly what to do regardless of in-app guidance. The instructor's briefing should always be the primary source of direction, not the app's UI.
feedback.b: Correct. When students don't know what they're looking for or what they should do, they wander. A proper briefing (Objective + Task) prevents this by giving a clear mission before headsets go on.
feedback.c: If multiple students are confused, the problem is systemic — either the briefing was unclear, too long, or missing key information. Blaming attention is less productive than improving briefing structure.

s1.statement: Briefing reduces student anxiety by providing clear expectations before entering VR
s1.explanation: True. Research from simulation-based education confirms that structured briefings reduce anxiety. When learners know the objective, task, duration, and exit options, uncertainty drops and cognitive resources shift to learning.

s2.statement: A thorough briefing should explain every feature of the VR application in detail
s2.explanation: False. Over-briefing kills curiosity and wastes time. The briefing should cover the learning objective, the task, rules, and time — not a full technical walkthrough. Keep hardware orientation under 30 seconds.

s3.statement: The O-R-T-T framework stands for Objective, Rules, Time, and Task
s3.explanation: True. O-R-T-T is the structured briefing framework: Objective (what to achieve), Rules (boundaries and safety), Time (duration), Task (specific actions). It ensures every briefing covers the essentials without overloading.

exercise.instruction: Classify each briefing item: should you explain it, let students discover it, or never mention it?
exercise.i1: The learning objective — what they will achieve
exercise.i2: Safety rules — how to signal discomfort or exit
exercise.i3: The specific task they need to perform
exercise.i4: What the virtual environment looks like inside
exercise.i5: Every interactive object they can touch or use
exercise.i7: Irrelevant app features unrelated to the objective
exercise.i8: Success criteria — how they'll know they did it right

scenario.context: You're writing a briefing for a VR chemistry lab simulation. A colleague suggests you include: the learning objective, all 15 interactive objects in the lab, the correct procedure for the experiment, safety rules, and a walkthrough of the VR menu system.
scenario.question: What's wrong with this briefing plan?

choices.a: Nothing — comprehensive briefings prevent confusion
choices.b: It includes too much: listing all objects and the correct procedure removes the challenge. Brief the objective, task, safety rules, and success criteria — let students discover the environment and solve the procedure themselves
choices.c: Drop the menu walkthrough but keep everything else

feedback.a: Over-briefing is as problematic as under-briefing. Listing every interactive object and the correct procedure removes discovery and challenge — the very things that make VR learning effective.
feedback.b: Correct. A briefing should focus on the objective, task, rules, and criteria. The environment details and problem-solving process are what students should discover through the VR experience. Giving them the answers in advance defeats the purpose.
feedback.c: The menu walkthrough is one problem, but the bigger issue is listing all 15 objects and the correct procedure. This over-briefing removes the learning challenge entirely. Students should discover the environment and figure out the process.

c1.context: A teacher gives a 7-minute briefing before a 10-minute VR experience. They explain every room, every object, and exactly what students will see. Students put on headsets and complete the task quickly but seem bored.
c1.question: Why did the VR experience fall flat?

choices.a: The VR experience was too short — students needed more time
choices.b: The headsets weren't configured properly
choices.c: The briefing revealed too much — there was nothing left to discover. Over-briefing killed the curiosity and challenge that make VR engaging

feedback.a: Duration wasn't the issue — 10 minutes is a reasonable VR session length. The problem is that students had no surprises or challenges left because the briefing gave everything away.
feedback.b: Headset configuration doesn't explain boredom. The students completed the task fine — they just weren't engaged because the briefing removed all discovery.
