feedback.c: Exactly. When a briefing reveals the entire environment and solution, the VR experience becomes confirmation, not exploration. Students need enough direction to focus but enough mystery to stay curious.

c2.context: A teacher gives a 15-second briefing: 'Put on headsets and do the lab activity.' Three minutes into the session, students are calling out confused questions.
c2.question: What's the root cause?

choices.a: The briefing was too short and missed critical information — students don't know the objective, what to do, or how long they have. Under-briefing causes wasted VR time.
choices.b: The students aren't used to VR technology yet
choices.c: The VR app should have included better in-app instructions

feedback.a: Correct. 'Do the lab activity' tells students nothing about the objective, task, or success criteria. A complete briefing needs O-R-T-T: what to achieve, what rules apply, how long, and what to do. Under-briefing wastes VR time on confusion.
feedback.b: Technology familiarity is a factor, but even experienced VR users need a briefing with clear objectives. The confusion here is about what to do, not how to use the headset.
feedback.c: Relying on in-app instructions is risky — they might not cover the learning objective, and students in VR can easily miss or skip them. The instructor's briefing should always be the primary direction.

s1.statement: A good briefing should explain the step-by-step solution so students know exactly what to do
s1.explanation: False. Giving students the solution removes the learning challenge. A briefing explains the objective, task, and criteria — not the answer. Students should discover the solution through the VR experience.

s2.statement: Safety rules and exit procedures should always be included in a pre-VR briefing
s2.explanation: True. Safety is never optional. Students must always know how to signal discomfort, request to stop, or exit VR before entering the experience. This is part of the 'Rules' in O-R-T-T.

s3.statement: If a briefing takes longer than the VR experience itself, that's fine as long as it's thorough
s3.explanation: False. If your briefing is longer than the VR experience, you're over-briefing. A briefing should be concise (under 60 seconds ideally) and focus on essentials. Longer briefings indicate you're explaining too much.

exercise.instruction: Complete the 60-second briefing script by dragging the correct words into the blanks
exercise.template: Your __blank1__ is to identify all safety hazards in the virtual workspace. You will __blank2__ through each room and flag every hazard you find. The __blank3__ are: do not skip any room, and raise your hand if you feel uncomfortable. You have __blank4__ minutes. Begin when the environment loads.

scenario.context: The bell rings in 90 seconds. You have a VR hazard-identification session planned. Students are seated and ready but you haven't briefed them yet.
scenario.question: What do you do?

choices.a: Skip the briefing — there's no time. Hand out headsets and tell students to look for hazards
choices.b: Postpone VR to next class — a proper briefing takes at least 5 minutes
choices.c: Deliver a 60-second structured briefing: state the objective, describe the task and rules, set the time, and launch

feedback.a: Even with limited time, skipping the briefing means students don't know the specific task, success criteria, or safety rules. A 60-second briefing is always possible and always worth it.
feedback.b: A proper briefing does not need 5 minutes. The O-R-T-T framework is designed to work in 60 seconds: 15s objective, 25s task and rules, 20s time and start. Don't postpone when a structured template gets it done.
feedback.c: Exactly. The 60-second briefing template works under time pressure: Objective (15s) — 'Your goal is to find all safety hazards.' Task and Rules (25s) — 'Walk through each room, flag hazards, don't skip rooms.' Time and Start (20s) — 'You have 10 minutes. Begin now.'

c1.context: A teacher delivers this briefing: 'Today you'll identify hazards in a virtual warehouse. Walk through all three sections and flag every hazard using the pointer. Do not skip any section. If you feel dizzy, raise your hand. You have 8 minutes. Focus on accuracy. Start when the environment loads.'
c1.question: How would you rate this briefing?

choices.a: Excellent — it covers all four O-R-T-T elements (Objective, Rules, Time, Task) concisely and clearly, with a safety exit instruction included
choices.b: Good but too long — it should be shorter
choices.c: Missing important information — it doesn't explain the controller buttons

feedback.a: Exactly. This briefing hits every component: Objective (identify hazards), Task (walk through sections, flag hazards with pointer), Rules (don't skip sections, raise hand if dizzy), Time (8 minutes). It's concise, clear, and actionable.
feedback.b: This briefing is approximately 15 seconds — that's well within the 60-second target. It's not too long; it's efficient.
feedback.c: Controller details are hardware orientation, not briefing. Keep hardware instructions to 30 seconds before the briefing. The briefing itself focuses on the learning objective and task.

c2.context: A colleague's briefing: 'OK everyone, let's do VR now. The app is really cool, you'll see a warehouse. There's lots of stuff to find. Try to find as many things as you can. Have fun!'
c2.question: What's missing from this briefing?

choices.a: Enthusiasm — the teacher should sound more excited
choices.b: Almost everything: there's no clear objective, no specific task description, no rules, no time limit, and no success criteria. Students won't know what 'things' to find or how to know when they're done
choices.c: It's fine — the app will guide them once they're inside

feedback.a: The issue isn't tone — it's missing structure. 'Stuff to find' is not an objective. 'As many things as you can' is not a task with success criteria. Enthusiasm without structure leads to unfocused VR time.
feedback.b: Correct. This 'briefing' has none of the O-R-T-T components: no clear Objective (what are they finding? hazards? objects?), no Task (how?), no Rules (boundaries?), no Time (how long?). Students will wander without purpose.
feedback.c: Relying on in-app guidance is risky. The instructor's briefing provides the learning frame. Without it, students interact with the app but don't know what to focus on for learning.

cls.instruction: Which part of the 60-second briefing does each phrase belong to?
cls.i1: Your goal is to identify all safety hazards
cls.i2: Walk through each room and flag every hazard you find
cls.i3: Do not skip any room — check all three sections
cls.i5: Today you will practise the emergency evacuation procedure
cls.i6: If you feel uncomfortable at any point, raise your hand

exercise.instruction: Put the debriefing question phases in the correct order, from surface recall to deep application
exercise.i1: "What happened?" — Describe what you saw and experienced
exercise.i2: "What did you do?" — Explain your actions and decisions
exercise.i3: "What would you do differently?" — Reflect on alternatives
exercise.i4: "How does this apply to the real world?" — Transfer to practice
exercise.i5: Facilitator summarises key takeaways and next steps

scenario.context: Students just completed a VR fire evacuation simulation. They remove their headsets and look at you expectantly. You have 8 minutes left in the session.
scenario.question: How do you run the debriefing?

choices.a: Tell them what they did right and wrong, then dismiss them
choices.b: Ask 'Did you like it?' and let them chat informally about the experience
choices.c: Follow the GAS model: Gather what happened, Analyze why they made their decisions, then Summarize the key lessons and how they connect to real practice

feedback.a: Telling students what they did right and wrong is instructor lecturing, not debriefing. Effective debriefing is learner-centred — students analyse their own performance. The instructor asks questions, not delivers corrections.
feedback.b: 'Did you like it?' starts a conversation about entertainment, not learning. Informal chat can be engaging but doesn't extract educational value. A structured model ensures the conversation moves from description to analysis to transfer.
feedback.c: Correct. The GAS model (Gather-Analyze-Summarize) gives structure: first collect what happened, then examine why things went right or wrong, then draw conclusions and connect to real-world application. This transforms the VR experience into lasting learning.

c1.context: During debriefing, an instructor immediately says: 'You missed the fire extinguisher on the second floor. That was the most important item. Let me explain why...' and lectures for 4 minutes.
c1.question: What went wrong?

choices.a: Nothing — the instructor corrected an important mistake
choices.b: The instructor skipped the 'Gather' and 'Analyze' phases and jumped straight to telling. Effective debriefing asks learners to identify their own mistakes before the instructor provides corrections. The 80/20 rule applies: learners should talk 80% of the time.
choices.c: The instructor should have waited until the next class to give corrections

feedback.a: Correcting mistakes is important, but the method matters. Premature correction short-circuits reflection. When students discover their own errors through guided questions, the lesson is deeper and more durable.
feedback.b: Exactly. The instructor violated two principles: (1) they skipped gathering student observations first, and (2) they lectured instead of asking. Better approach: 'What did you find on the second floor?' → 'Was there anything you might have missed?' → students self-discover the gap.
feedback.c: Waiting too long to debrief weakens the connection to the experience. The timing was right — the method was wrong. Debrief immediately, but ask questions instead of lecturing.

c2.context: A teacher runs a debriefing using the four question phases. Students describe what happened (Phase 1), explain their decisions (Phase 2), and suggest improvements (Phase 3). Then the bell rings.
c2.question: What's the biggest loss from missing Phase 4?

choices.a: Nothing critical — three phases are enough for a good debrief
choices.b: The debrief was too slow and should have been faster to fit all phases
choices.c: Phase 4 ('How does this apply to the real world?') is the transfer step — without it, learning stays trapped in the virtual scenario. Students never connect VR decisions to real professional practice, which is the ultimate goal of simulation-based education.

feedback.a: Three phases create good reflection, but the transfer question is the most valuable. Without it, students may see the VR experience as isolated from their real work. If time runs short, prioritise the transfer question.
feedback.b: Speed isn't the solution — rushing phases produces shallow reflection. But if time is genuinely short, prioritise Phase 4 (transfer) because it has the highest learning impact. You can compress Phases 1–3 but should never skip Phase 4.
feedback.c: Correct. Phase 4 is where virtual learning becomes real-world capability. 'How would you apply this in a real fire evacuation?' forces students to bridge the gap between simulation and practice. If time is short, always prioritise the transfer question.

cls.instruction: Which phase of the GAS debriefing model does each activity belong to?
cls.i1: Ask students to describe what they saw and experienced
cls.i2: Examine why students made the decisions they did
cls.i3: State the key lessons and connect them to real practice
cls.i4: Collect observations: 'What happened first? What came next?'
cls.i5: Ask: 'Why did you choose that action instead of the alternatives?'
cls.i6: Conclude with: 'How will you apply this in your real work?'

exercise.instruction: Match each VR assessment area to the type of student performance it evaluates

p1.def: Did the student complete all procedure steps accurately and in the correct order?

p2.def: Did the student choose the right response when conditions changed unexpectedly?

p3.def: Did the student follow the protocol checklist without skipping or reordering steps?

p4.def: Did the student give clear instructions and stay calm under pressure?

scenario.context: After a VR emergency response simulation, you need to assess student performance. One student completed the evacuation fastest but skipped two safety checkpoints. Another was slower but followed every protocol step and communicated clearly with virtual teammates.
scenario.question: Who performed better?

choices.a: The fast student — speed and task completion are the most important metrics in an emergency
choices.b: Both performed equally — speed and thoroughness are equally valid approaches
choices.c: The thorough student — VR assessment should evaluate decision-making, procedure compliance, and communication, not just speed. The fast student missed safety steps, which in a real emergency would be critical errors.

feedback.a: Speed is one metric, but skipping safety checkpoints in an emergency is a critical failure. VR assessment lets you see not just whether students completed the task, but how they made decisions along the way.
feedback.b: In an emergency response scenario, thoroughness and protocol compliance are not equal to speed with shortcuts. Skipping safety steps in a real emergency has consequences. VR assessment should capture this distinction.
feedback.c: Correct. VR assessment captures four areas: skills (execution), decision-making (choices), procedures (compliance), and communication (clarity). A holistic view reveals that the 'slower' student actually demonstrated stronger professional competence across all four areas.

c1.context: A teacher only assesses VR performance based on whether students completed the simulation task (pass/fail). They ignore how students made decisions, whether they followed the correct procedure, and how they communicated.
c1.question: What's the problem with this assessment approach?

choices.a: It misses three of the four assessment areas. Task completion (skills) is only one dimension. Decision-making quality, procedure compliance, and communication are equally important — and VR uniquely reveals all four.
choices.b: Pass/fail is actually the best approach for VR — it's simple and objective
choices.c: The teacher should add a written test alongside the VR assessment

feedback.a: Exactly. VR's unique advantage is that it captures the full process, not just the outcome. A student who completes a task but makes poor decisions, skips steps, or communicates poorly would fail in a real scenario. Assessing all four areas gives a complete picture.
feedback.b: Pass/fail captures outcomes but misses the how. Two students can both 'pass' with very different decision quality, procedure adherence, and communication. VR gives you visibility into the process — use it.
feedback.c: A written test assesses knowledge, not performance. VR already captures skills, decisions, procedures, and communication in action. Adding a test duplicates what VR observation already reveals — focus on observing the four assessment areas during VR instead.

c2.context: During a VR surgical preparation simulation, a nursing student performs every step correctly but never verbalises what they're doing. In a real operating room, verbal communication of each step is mandatory for safety.
c2.question: How should you assess this student?

choices.a: Full marks — they performed all steps correctly and that's what matters
choices.b: Strong in skills and procedures, but weak in communication. The assessment should note that the student's technical execution was excellent but that verbal communication — a critical professional behaviour in this context — needs development.
choices.c: Fail the student — communication is more important than technical accuracy

feedback.a: Technical accuracy is excellent, but in a context where verbal communication is a safety requirement (surgical prep), silent execution is a gap. VR assessment should capture all relevant areas, not just task completion.
feedback.b: Correct. VR assessment lets you identify specific strengths and gaps across all four areas. This student excels in skills and procedures but needs work on communication. This nuanced feedback is far more useful than a simple pass/fail.
feedback.c: Failing the student ignores their genuine strengths. The goal of assessment is to identify specific areas for improvement, not to apply all-or-nothing judgments. Skills and procedures were strong — communication needs targeted practice.

cls.instruction: Which VR assessment area does each observation belong to?
cls.i1: Student completed all steps of the injection technique in the correct order
cls.i2: Student changed their approach when the virtual patient's condition worsened
cls.i3: Student followed the safety checklist without skipping any items
cls.i4: Student clearly announced each action to the virtual team
cls.i5: Student chose to evacuate when conditions became dangerous instead of continuing the task

exercise.instruction: Walk through the WiFi troubleshooting steps. At each stage, choose the best action.
exercise.scenario: A student reports their headset shows 'No WiFi connection' during a VR lesson. You need to diagnose and fix the problem.

n1.prompt: The headset displays 'No WiFi connection'. What do you check first?

a.label: Open Quick Settings and check if WiFi is toggled on
a.feedback: Correct. The simplest check first — WiFi may have been accidentally toggled off. Quick Settings is the fastest way to verify.

b.label: Immediately restart the headset
b.feedback: Restarting works but skips simpler checks. Always verify WiFi is toggled on first — it takes 5 seconds and solves many cases.

c.label: Factory reset the headset to clear all settings
c.feedback: Factory reset is a last resort that wipes all data. Never start here — check the WiFi toggle first.

n2.prompt: WiFi is enabled but the network keeps dropping after a few seconds.

a.label: Switch the headset to airplane mode and back
a.feedback: Airplane mode toggles all radios, but it's slower and less targeted than forgetting the specific network.

b.label: Forget the network and re-enter the password
b.feedback: Correct. Forgetting and rejoining clears cached connection data that may be corrupted. This resolves most persistent drop issues.

c.label: Move to a different classroom with better WiFi
c.feedback: Changing rooms is disruptive and rarely necessary. The issue is usually the connection profile, not the signal.

n3.prompt: The student reconnected, but WiFi drops again after 2 minutes.

a.label: Restart the headset — a full reboot clears deeper network state
a.feedback: Correct. When toggle and rejoin don't work, a restart clears the network stack. If it still fails after restart, the issue is likely the school network, not the headset.

b.label: Continue without WiFi — the VR app might still work offline
b.feedback: Some apps work offline, but many require a network connection. Restarting is more reliable than hoping the app works without WiFi.

scenario.context: Mid-lesson, 3 of 8 headsets simultaneously lose WiFi. Students are in the middle of a VR science simulation. Five headsets are still working fine.
scenario.question: What do you do first?

choices.a: Stop the entire class and troubleshoot all 3 headsets together
choices.b: Have the 3 affected students toggle WiFi off/on in Quick Settings while the rest continue. If not fixed in 2 minutes, rotate them to working headsets.
choices.c: Call IT support immediately — simultaneous drops suggest a network problem beyond your control

feedback.a: Stopping the entire class wastes learning time for the 5 students whose headsets work fine. Let them continue while you address the issue with the affected students.
feedback.b: Exactly right. Quick WiFi toggle is the fastest fix. The 2-minute rule keeps the lesson moving — if the toggle doesn't work, rotate students so everyone gets VR time. Troubleshoot the headsets after class.
feedback.c: IT support may be needed eventually, but basic troubleshooting (WiFi toggle, restart) takes under 2 minutes and solves most issues. Always try the quick fixes first.

c1.context: A student's headset connects to WiFi but keeps dropping every few minutes. You've already toggled WiFi and restarted the headset.
c1.question: What should you try next?

choices.a: Forget the network entirely and re-enter the WiFi password from scratch
choices.b: Move the student closer to the projector screen
choices.c: Check if the student's phone is creating wireless interference

feedback.a: Correct. Forgetting and rejoining the network clears cached credentials and connection profiles. This is the standard next step when toggle and restart have already failed.
feedback.b: The projector screen has no relationship to WiFi signal strength. This won't help.
feedback.c: Phone interference is extremely rare with modern WiFi. This is theoretically possible but almost never the actual cause.

c2.context: No headsets in the classroom can connect to WiFi at all. Other devices like phones and laptops work fine on the same network.
c2.question: What is the most likely cause?

choices.a: All headsets have broken WiFi hardware simultaneously
choices.b: The school network is blocking VR headset traffic — MAC address filtering, device restrictions, or a captive portal the headsets cannot navigate
choices.c: The classroom is too far from the WiFi router

feedback.a: Multiple headsets failing simultaneously with identical symptoms is almost never a hardware issue. It points to an external cause like network restrictions.
feedback.b: Correct. When personal devices connect but VR headsets cannot, the issue is network policy — not hardware. School IT may have MAC filtering, device-type blocking, or a captive portal that headsets cannot display. Test with a phone hotspot to confirm the headsets themselves work.
feedback.c: If phones work fine in the same location, distance is not the issue. The signal reaches the room — the headsets are being blocked at the network level.

s1.statement: Toggling WiFi off and back on is often enough to resolve a dropped connection
s1.explanation: True. A simple WiFi toggle is the fastest fix and resolves the majority of connection drops. It forces the headset to re-establish the connection without a full restart.

s2.statement: If a headset cannot connect to the school WiFi, the headset's WiFi hardware is definitely broken
s2.explanation: False. School networks frequently block unfamiliar devices via MAC filtering, device restrictions, or captive portals. Test with a phone hotspot first — if the headset connects to the hotspot, the hardware is fine and the issue is the school network.

s3.statement: Testing with a phone hotspot is a good way to confirm whether the headset's WiFi hardware works
s3.explanation: True. A phone hotspot bypasses school network policies entirely. If the headset connects successfully, you've confirmed the hardware is fine and isolated the problem to the school network.

exercise.instruction: Put the Boundary troubleshooting steps in the correct order, from easiest to most drastic
exercise.i1: Check room lighting — ensure even, consistent illumination with no dark corners
exercise.i2: Cover or face away from mirrors and reflective surfaces
exercise.i3: Redraw the Boundary from scratch in Settings
exercise.i4: Clean the headset's external cameras with a dry microfibre cloth
exercise.i5: Switch to Stationary Boundary mode as a fallback

scenario.context: Students put on headsets but 4 of them are stuck on the Boundary setup screen. They cannot proceed. The classroom has fluorescent lights and one wall is a full-length mirror.
scenario.question: What is the most likely cause and fix?

choices.a: The headsets need a software update to fix a Boundary bug
choices.b: The fluorescent lighting may be causing flicker and the mirror is confusing the tracking cameras — turn on additional steady lights and have students face away from the mirror or cover it
choices.c: Tell students to skip the Boundary by choosing Stationary mode

feedback.a: Boundary issues are almost always caused by the physical environment, not software bugs. Firmware updates rarely fix tracking problems. Check the room conditions first.
feedback.b: Exactly. Fluorescent flicker and mirror reflections are the two most common Boundary killers. Adding steady lighting and eliminating reflective surfaces resolves the root cause for this session and all future ones.
feedback.c: Stationary mode avoids the full Boundary but also removes the safety zone. It fixes the symptom without addressing the cause — the same problem will recur every session until you fix the environment.

c1.context: A student's Boundary keeps resetting every 30 seconds, even though the room is well-lit with no mirrors. You have already redrawn the boundary twice.
c1.question: What should you try?

choices.a: Clean the headset's external cameras with a dry microfibre cloth — smudges or fingerprints degrade tracking quality
choices.b: Restart the WiFi router — the Boundary might depend on network connectivity
choices.c: Tell the student to just stay seated and avoid moving

feedback.a: Correct. Dirty cameras are an often-overlooked cause of persistent tracking failures. Students handle headsets constantly, leaving fingerprints on the external cameras. A quick wipe with a dry microfibre cloth can restore tracking.
feedback.b: The Boundary system uses the headset's cameras for spatial tracking — it does not use WiFi. Restarting the router will not affect boundary behaviour.
feedback.c: Sitting still avoids the problem but prevents the student from using room-scale VR. Fix the root cause — clean the cameras — so the student can move freely.

c2.context: You are setting up a VR session in a new room. The floor is a uniform dark grey carpet and the ceiling has dim recessed lighting. The Boundary refuses to initialise on any headset.
c2.question: What are two things that would help?

choices.a: Place contrasting objects on the floor and add brighter portable lights — the cameras need visual features and illumination to map the space
choices.b: Use a different VR app that does not require the Boundary
choices.c: Switch all headsets to developer mode to bypass the Boundary

feedback.a: Correct. The cameras need two things: enough light to see, and enough visual contrast to identify features. A uniform dark surface with dim lighting gives the cameras nothing to track. Adding objects (even tape lines on the floor) and portable lights solves both problems.
feedback.b: Most VR apps require some form of spatial tracking. Avoiding the Boundary does not eliminate the need for the cameras to see the environment.
feedback.c: Developer mode is not a standard classroom solution and may introduce other issues. The correct fix is to improve the physical environment for the cameras.

s1.statement: Dim lighting is the most common cause of Boundary failures
s1.explanation: True. The headset cameras rely on visible light to detect the environment. Dim, uneven, or flickering lighting prevents accurate spatial mapping and is the single most frequent cause of Boundary problems.

s2.statement: Mirrors and reflective surfaces help the Boundary cameras by providing more visual reference points
s2.explanation: False. Mirrors create false reflections that confuse the cameras. The tracking system interprets reflections as real physical space, causing boundaries to shift, reset, or fail entirely. Always cover mirrors or face students away from them.

s3.statement: Clearing Boundary History in Settings and redrawing the boundary is a standard fix for persistent tracking issues
s3.explanation: True. Old boundary data can become corrupted, especially if the room layout has changed. Clearing the saved mapping and redrawing from scratch gives the cameras a fresh reference and resolves many persistent issues.

exercise.instruction: Walk through the power diagnosis steps. At each stage, choose the best approach.
exercise.scenario: A headset will not turn on. No display, no LED, no sound. You need to diagnose the problem.

n1.prompt: The headset shows no sign of life. What do you check first?

a.label: Check the charging LED — plug it in and look for the indicator light
a.feedback: Good approach. The LED is the fastest diagnostic: orange means charging, green means full, no LED means power is not reaching the headset. This tells you exactly where to look next.

b.label: Hold the power button for a full 3 seconds — brief taps may not register
b.feedback: Reasonable — a common mistake is tapping the power button instead of holding it. But checking the charging LED first gives you more diagnostic information.

c.label: Perform a force restart immediately — hold power + volume-down for 30 seconds
c.feedback: A force restart is a useful tool, but it is overkill as a first step. You do not yet know if the problem is charging, power delivery, or a frozen OS. Start with simpler checks.

n2.prompt: No LED appears even when plugged in. The headset shows no response to the charger.

a.label: Try a different USB-C cable and a different power adapter
a.feedback: Good approach. Damaged cables and underpowered adapters are the most common and most underdiagnosed cause of charging failures. Swapping the cable often solves the problem immediately.

b.label: Clean the USB-C charging port with compressed air
b.feedback: A dirty port can cause charging issues, but it is less common than a faulty cable. Try a different cable first — it is faster and more likely to be the culprit.

c.label: Set the headset aside and use a different one — this one is probably broken
c.feedback: Giving up too early. A headset with no charging LED is almost always a cable or adapter problem, not a hardware failure. Try a different cable before declaring it broken.

n3.endFeedback: The correct diagnostic sequence is: check the charging LED → try the power button for 3 seconds → swap the USB-C cable and adapter → clean the port → force restart → set aside for post-class investigation. Most 'dead' headsets are simply not receiving power due to a faulty cable.

scenario.context: It is 5 minutes before class. You press the power button on a headset and nothing happens — no display, no LED, no sound. You have 10 headsets but need all of them for today's paired activity.
scenario.question: What is your first action?

choices.a: Try a force restart immediately — hold power + volume-down for 30 seconds
choices.b: Plug it into the charger, check for a charging LED, and try a different USB-C cable if no LED appears — while waiting, reorganise the activity to work with 9 headsets
choices.c: Set it aside immediately and plan to send it for repair

feedback.a: A force restart is useful for a frozen headset, but with no LED and no response, the problem is more likely power delivery than a frozen OS. Check the charging path first.
feedback.b: Exactly right. Checking the charging LED and swapping cables is the fastest path to diagnosis. Meanwhile, adapting the activity to 9 headsets keeps the lesson on track. The troubleshooting mindset: fix what you can quickly, adapt the plan, deal with broken equipment after class.
feedback.c: This is almost certainly fixable. Most 'dead' headsets have a simple cable or charging issue. Setting it aside without trying basic steps wastes a perfectly good headset.

c1.context: A headset shows an orange charging LED when plugged in but still will not turn on after 5 minutes of charging. The power button produces no response at all.
c1.question: What should you try?

choices.a: Hold the power button for a full 3 seconds — brief taps do not always register on some headsets
choices.b: The battery is too drained and needs overnight charging — there is nothing you can do now
choices.c: The headset is broken and needs a factory reset through the boot menu

feedback.a: Correct. A common mistake is tapping the power button quickly instead of holding it for a full 3 seconds. With the orange LED confirming power delivery, a sustained button press is the most likely fix.
feedback.b: While severely drained batteries do take time, 5 minutes of charging with an orange LED usually provides enough power to boot. Try holding the power button first.
feedback.c: A factory reset would erase all data and is unnecessary here. The orange LED confirms the headset is receiving power — the issue is likely just the power button press duration.

c2.context: A headset turns on, shows the Meta logo, then immediately shuts off. This boot loop cycle repeats. The battery indicator briefly shows 45% charge.
c2.question: This is likely what kind of problem, and what do you do?

choices.a: A WiFi authentication error — reconnect to the network
choices.b: A frozen operating system — perform a force restart by holding power + volume-down for 30 seconds to break the boot loop
choices.c: Low battery despite what the indicator shows — plug it in and wait

feedback.a: WiFi issues cannot cause a boot loop. The headset is failing during startup, before it even reaches network connectivity. This is an OS or firmware problem.
feedback.b: Correct. A boot loop (power on → logo → shutdown → repeat) indicates the operating system is crashing during startup. A force restart (power + volume-down for 30 seconds) forces a clean boot that usually breaks the cycle.
feedback.c: The battery shows 45%, which is more than enough to boot. Boot loops are caused by software crashes, not low battery. A force restart is the correct fix.

cls.instruction: What does each LED indicator on a charging headset mean?
cls.i1: Solid orange LED
cls.i2: Solid green LED
cls.i3: No LED at all despite being plugged in

exercise.instruction: Classify each casting problem by its root cause: is it a network problem, a compatibility problem, or a settings problem?
exercise.i1: Headset and TV are connected to different WiFi networks
exercise.i2: Casting to a non-Chromecast smart TV that lacks built-in Cast support
exercise.i3: AP isolation is enabled on the school router, blocking device-to-device traffic
exercise.i4: Using Firefox instead of Chrome for browser-based casting at horizon.meta.com/casting
exercise.i5: Cast target device does not appear in the headset's Cast menu
exercise.i6: Headset is on the 2.4 GHz band while the TV is on the 5 GHz band
exercise.i7: Cast was started from the TV side instead of from the headset's Quick Settings
exercise.i8: No USB screen-mirroring app installed as a wired fallback option

scenario.context: You want to cast a student's VR view to the classroom TV so everyone can watch. You start casting from the headset but the TV does not appear as an option in the Cast menu.
scenario.question: What are the first two things to check?

choices.a: Update the headset firmware — old firmware breaks casting functionality
choices.b: Verify both devices are on the same WiFi network, and confirm the TV is a Chromecast-enabled device or has a Chromecast plugged in
choices.c: Restart the TV and try again

feedback.a: Firmware updates rarely affect casting discovery. The most common causes are network mismatches and device compatibility — check those first.
feedback.b: Correct. The two most common reasons a Cast target does not appear: (1) the devices are on different WiFi networks, and (2) the TV does not support the Cast protocol. Same network + Chromecast compatibility covers 80% of casting failures.
feedback.c: Restarting is worth trying but does not address the two most likely root causes. Check network and compatibility first for a targeted fix.

c1.context: Casting starts successfully from headset to TV, but the image freezes after 30 seconds and then disconnects entirely. Both devices are on the same WiFi network.
c1.question: What is the most likely cause?

choices.a: The VR app does not support casting
choices.b: Network congestion or AP isolation — the router may be throttling or blocking device-to-device traffic on the school network
choices.c: The headset battery is too low to sustain a casting stream

feedback.a: Some apps restrict casting, but that would prevent casting from starting at all, not cause it to freeze mid-stream. Freezing and disconnecting points to a network issue.
feedback.b: Correct. When casting starts but degrades, the network is the culprit. AP isolation blocks or throttles device-to-device traffic. Network congestion from many devices also causes freezing. Ask IT about isolation settings or try a dedicated router.
feedback.c: Battery level does not affect casting quality. Low battery would cause the headset to shut down, not the cast to freeze.

c2.context: No Chromecast or smart TV is available in your classroom. You still need to show the VR view to students who are not wearing headsets.
c2.question: What is your best fallback?

choices.a: Use a screen-mirroring app on a laptop connected to the headset via USB as a wired fallback
choices.b: Have students take turns looking through the headset while others wait
choices.c: Cancel the shared viewing and have students describe what they see verbally to each other

feedback.a: Correct. A USB cable and a screen-mirroring app on a laptop bypasses WiFi and casting entirely. Connect the headset to the laptop, project the laptop screen, and the whole class sees the VR view with zero network dependency.
feedback.b: Taking turns is extremely slow with a full class and wastes most students' time. A wired connection is far more practical.
feedback.c: Verbal descriptions lose the visual impact of shared VR viewing. If you cannot cast wirelessly, a wired USB connection is a reliable alternative.

s1.statement: The headset and the TV or Chromecast must be on the same WiFi network for casting to work
s1.explanation: True. Casting uses local network communication between devices. If the headset is on one WiFi network and the TV or Chromecast is on another, they cannot discover or communicate with each other.

s2.statement: Casting should always be started from the TV or Chromecast side, not the headset
s2.explanation: False. Always start casting from the headset: Quick Settings → Cast → select the target device. Starting from the TV side is unreliable and not the supported workflow for Meta Quest headsets.

s3.statement: If casting does not work wirelessly, a USB connection to a laptop with a screen-mirroring app is a reliable fallback
s3.explanation: True. A USB wired connection bypasses all WiFi and casting issues. Screen-mirroring apps display the headset view on the laptop screen, which can then be projected for the class.

exercise.instruction: Is each statement a fact or a myth about troubleshooting VR in classrooms?

s1.statement: You should spend as long as needed to fix a broken headset during class
s1.explanation: Myth. The 2-minute rule exists to protect learning time. If a headset is not fixed in 2 minutes, set it aside, rotate students to working headsets, and troubleshoot after class.

s2.statement: Having a non-VR backup activity ready is a sign of poor preparation
s2.explanation: Myth. A Plan B shows excellent preparation. Technology fails unpredictably — having a non-VR activity that covers the same learning objective means the lesson succeeds regardless of tech issues.

s3.statement: Most VR headset issues in classrooms can be resolved with a simple restart
s3.explanation: Fact. Restarting clears temporary software glitches, resets network connections, and resolves many common issues. It is the single most effective general-purpose fix after basic checks.

s4.statement: If some headsets break during a session, rotate students through the working ones rather than stopping the class
s4.explanation: Fact. Rotation keeps every student learning. If 3 of 12 headsets fail, reorganise into groups that cycle through the 9 working headsets. Every student still gets VR time, and you fix the broken ones later.

s5.statement: A VR lesson where the technology fails is a failed lesson
s5.explanation: Myth. The learning objective matters more than the technology. A teacher who adapts to equipment failure — using backup activities, rotating headsets, staying calm — delivers a successful lesson regardless of tech problems.

scenario.context: Three headsets malfunction at the start of a VR lesson. You have 30 students and planned to use all 15 headsets in pairs. Students are watching you expectantly.
scenario.question: What do you do?

choices.a: Spend 10 minutes fixing the headsets while students wait — you cannot run VR with fewer headsets
choices.b: Stay calm, acknowledge the issue briefly, reorganise into groups of 3 with the 12 working headsets, and rotate so everyone gets VR time. Note the broken headsets for post-class troubleshooting.
choices.c: Cancel the VR session entirely and switch to a textbook activity

feedback.a: Ten minutes of idle time for 30 students is a significant learning loss. The 2-minute rule says: if it is not fixed in 2 minutes, adapt the plan and keep teaching.
feedback.b: Exactly right. Staying calm models composure. Reorganising into groups of 3 means all students still get VR time with the 12 working headsets. The broken headsets are a post-class problem, not a mid-lesson emergency.
feedback.c: Cancelling the entire session because 3 of 15 headsets failed is an overreaction. Twelve working headsets is more than enough to run a successful VR lesson with adapted grouping.

c1.context: A teacher spends 15 minutes trying to fix one headset while 29 students sit idle. The teacher mutters 'These things never work' and visibly shows frustration.
c1.question: What is wrong with this approach?

choices.a: The teacher is right to prioritise fixing the technology — you cannot teach VR without it
choices.b: The teacher violated three principles: the 2-minute troubleshooting limit, staying calm in front of students, and prioritising learning over technology. Students absorbed 15 minutes of frustration instead of learning.
choices.c: The teacher should have called IT support instead of trying to fix it themselves

feedback.a: You can absolutely teach a VR lesson with one fewer headset. The teacher prioritised one device over the learning of 29 students — this is the exact mistake the 2-minute rule prevents.
feedback.b: Correct. Three mistakes compounded: (1) spending 15 minutes on a single headset far exceeds the 2-minute limit, (2) visible frustration tells students that VR is unreliable and stressful, and (3) 29 students lost learning time because one device was prioritised over the lesson.
feedback.c: IT support may help, but the bigger issue is the teacher's response. A calm teacher would have set the headset aside after 2 minutes, adapted the plan, and continued teaching.

c2.context: An experienced VR teacher arrives early, tests all headsets, and discovers one is stuck on a software update. Instead of panicking, they set it aside, adjust their plan to use one fewer headset, and prepare their usual paper-based backup activity just in case.
c2.question: What principle does this demonstrate?

choices.a: That VR is unreliable and teachers should always have non-VR alternatives
choices.b: Good time management — arriving early is the key
choices.c: The troubleshooting mindset: prepare for failures, test in advance, have a Plan B, and keep the focus on the learning objective rather than the technology

feedback.a: VR is not inherently unreliable — but all technology can fail. The principle is preparation, not avoidance. This teacher uses VR confidently because they are prepared for problems.
feedback.b: Arriving early helped, but the real principle is broader: testing equipment, adapting the plan without stress, and having a backup activity. This is the troubleshooting mindset.
feedback.c: Correct. This teacher demonstrates every element of the troubleshooting mindset: (1) test in advance, (2) stay calm when problems arise, (3) adapt the plan quickly, (4) have a backup activity ready, and (5) keep the learning objective as the priority, not the technology.

cls.instruction: Is this a good troubleshooting practice, a common mistake, or a Plan B strategy?
cls.i1: Spending 10+ minutes fixing one headset during class while students wait
cls.i2: Rotating students through fewer working headsets so everyone still gets VR time
cls.i3: Preparing a discussion activity that covers the same learning objective without VR
cls.i4: Showing visible frustration when technology fails in front of students
cls.i5: Testing all headsets before students arrive each session
cls.i6: Having a short video clip ready that covers the same topic as the VR experience

exercise.instruction: Label each statement as Fact or Myth based on what defines a genuine educational VR app.

s1.statement: A VR app that lets students fly through the solar system with no tasks, no feedback, and no assessment qualifies as educational.
s1.explanation: Myth. Without learning objectives, learner action, feedback, or measurable outcomes, this is a viewing experience — not an educational app.

s2.statement: An educational VR app must produce measurable learning outcomes that can be observed, scored, or recorded.
s2.explanation: Fact. Measurable outcomes are one of the four defining traits of an educational VR app, allowing instructors to verify that learning occurred.

s3.statement: A beautiful 360-degree video of a coral reef automatically qualifies as an educational VR app.
s3.explanation: Myth. A 360 video is passive viewing — the learner does not act, receive feedback, or demonstrate learning. Beauty does not equal education.

s4.statement: If a VR app requires the learner to perform actions and provides feedback on those actions, it meets two of the four educational criteria.
s4.explanation: Fact. Learner action and feedback are two of the four traits. The app would still need clear learning objectives and measurable outcomes to be fully educational.

s5.statement: High student engagement with a VR app always indicates that meaningful learning is taking place.
s5.explanation: Myth. Engagement and learning are not the same. Students can be highly engaged with entertainment content that produces no measurable learning.

scenario.context: Your department head wants to purchase a popular VR game for the classroom. Students who tried it during a demo day were thrilled and highly engaged. However, the app has no learning objectives, no feedback mechanism, and no way to track student performance.
scenario.question: What do you recommend?

choices.a: Buy it — student engagement is the most important factor, and the enthusiasm proves it works
choices.b: Explain that despite the engagement, the app lacks the four traits of an educational app and suggest evaluating alternatives that include learning objectives, learner action, feedback, and measurable outcomes
choices.c: Reject it outright — if it is a game, it cannot have educational value under any circumstances

feedback.a: Engagement matters, but it is not sufficient. An app without learning objectives, feedback, or measurable outcomes is entertainment, not education — no matter how excited students are.
feedback.b: Correct. You acknowledge the engagement value while pointing out the missing educational criteria. Suggesting alternatives with the four traits is constructive and evidence-based.
feedback.c: Too absolute. Games can be educational if they incorporate learning objectives, feedback, and measurable outcomes. The issue is not the format but the absence of educational design.

c1.context: A science teacher uses a VR app where students can rotate and zoom into a 3D model of a human cell. Students can tap organelles to see labels and short descriptions. There is no quiz, no task, and no feedback on student actions.
c1.question: Does this app meet the criteria for an educational VR app?

choices.a: It partially meets the criteria — it has implicit learning objectives (anatomy) and some learner action (tapping), but lacks feedback and measurable outcomes. It is closer to an interactive reference than a full educational app.
choices.b: Yes — the 3D model and labels make it fully educational
choices.c: No — it has zero educational value because there is no quiz

feedback.a: Correct. The app has elements of education but falls short on feedback and measurable outcomes. It could be enhanced with tasks like 'identify three organelles involved in energy production' to become truly educational.
feedback.b: Labels and interactivity are a start, but without feedback on learner actions and measurable outcomes, this is more of a reference tool than a complete educational app.
feedback.c: A quiz is one way to measure outcomes, but not the only one. The issue is broader — the app lacks feedback and structured tasks, not just a quiz.

c2.context: Two VR apps are available for teaching fire safety. App A shows a dramatic 360-degree video of a building fire — students watch from a fixed position. App B places students inside a burning building where they must locate exits, use fire extinguishers, and evacuate — the app scores their response time and decisions.
c2.question: Which app is educational according to the four criteria?

choices.a: App A — the dramatic footage creates a stronger emotional impact that students will remember
choices.b: App B — it requires learner action, provides feedback through scoring, has clear objectives (safe evacuation), and produces measurable outcomes
choices.c: Both are educational — they just use different approaches to teach fire safety

feedback.a: Emotional impact aids memory, but App A is passive viewing with no action, feedback, or measurable outcomes. It fails three of the four criteria.
feedback.b: Correct. App B meets all four criteria: clear learning objectives, required learner action, feedback via scoring, and measurable outcomes. This is the definition of an educational VR app.
feedback.c: App A is a viewing experience, not an educational app. Having educational subject matter does not automatically make an app educational — the design must include all four criteria.

s1.statement: An app must require the learner to take action — not just watch — to qualify as educational.
s1.explanation: True. Learner action is one of the four essential traits. Passive viewing, no matter how immersive, is not education.

s2.statement: Any VR app used in a classroom setting is automatically educational.
s2.explanation: False. The context of use does not change the app's design. An entertainment app used in a classroom is still entertainment.

s3.statement: Feedback from the app — showing consequences or corrections — is essential for learning.
s3.explanation: True. Without feedback, learners cannot know whether their actions were correct or how to improve.

b2.title: Where to Find Educational VR Apps

exercise.instruction: Match each distribution channel with its key characteristic.

p1.right: Curated and reviewed; mostly commercial titles with limited educational content

p2.right: Lower publishing barrier; where many educational VR apps are published in the Meta Horizon Store

p3.right: Custom-built and distributed internally by universities, hospitals, or training organisations

p4.right: Browser-based VR accessed via URL; no installation required

p5.right: Third-party platform for sideloaded content; requires developer mode enabled

scenario.context: A teacher searches the official Meta Store for VR clinical training apps and finds nothing. They tell the department that VR clinical simulation simply does not exist for their subject area.
scenario.question: What is wrong with this conclusion?

choices.a: Nothing — if the Meta Store does not have it, the app probably does not exist
choices.b: The teacher only checked one search — many educational VR apps are listed under Early Access in the Meta Horizon Store (formerly App Lab). They should also check institutional apps and WebXR options.
choices.c: The teacher should build their own custom VR app to solve the problem

feedback.a: The Meta Store is just one distribution channel, and it has limited educational content due to its curation requirements. Most educational VR apps are published elsewhere.
feedback.b: Correct. The Early Access section of the Meta Horizon Store (formerly App Lab) is where many educational VR apps live. Institutional channels and WebXR experiences are additional sources the teacher has not explored.
feedback.c: Building custom apps is expensive and time-consuming. Before going that route, the teacher should exhaust all existing distribution channels.

c1.context: A school wants to use a VR anatomy app developed by a medical university. The app is not on the Meta Horizon Store — it is distributed directly by the university via an APK file.
c1.question: What type of distribution channel is this?

choices.a: This is a private/institutional app — distributed internally by the developing organisation, typically via direct installation
choices.b: This is a WebXR experience since it is not on official stores
choices.c: This is a SideQuest app that the university uploaded

feedback.a: Correct. Private/institutional apps are custom-built by organisations and distributed through their own channels, often requiring direct installation via APK.
feedback.b: WebXR runs in the browser — this app requires installation via APK, making it a private/institutional distribution.
feedback.c: SideQuest is a third-party platform. Institutional apps distributed directly by the developer use their own private channel.

c2.context: A chemistry teacher finds a VR periodic table experience that runs entirely in the Meta Quest browser via a URL. No app installation is needed.
c2.question: What distribution channel does this represent?

choices.a: Early Access — since it is accessible without the main store review
choices.b: WebXR — browser-based VR experiences that require no installation, accessed via URL inside the headset browser
choices.c: This is not a real distribution channel — browser-based VR does not exist

feedback.a: Early Access apps still require installation. This experience runs directly in the browser with no installation.
feedback.b: Correct. WebXR experiences are browser-based VR that run via URL, making them the easiest to deploy in classrooms — no installation, no updates, no storage concerns.
feedback.c: WebXR is a well-established technology. Browser-based VR experiences are increasingly common for educational use.

cls.instruction: Classify each item by its access requirements.
cls.i1: Early Access apps shared via link
cls.i2: University-developed APK distributed internally
cls.i3: WebXR experience via public URL
cls.i5: Hospital training app on private MDM system

exercise.instruction: You are a nursing instructor choosing VR for clinical skills training. Navigate through the decisions to build the best learning path.
exercise.scenario: Your nursing students need to learn patient assessment skills. You have access to three types of VR apps: exploration (virtual hospital tour), guided training (step-by-step assessment protocol), and simulation (open patient assessment with consequences).

n1.prompt: Your students have never performed a patient assessment before. Which VR experience do you select first?

a.label: Exploration app — let them tour the virtual hospital to get familiar with the environment
a.feedback: A reasonable starting point for orientation, but exploration alone does not teach the assessment procedure. Students need structured instruction next.

b.label: Guided training app — walk them through the assessment protocol step by step with feedback
b.feedback: Excellent choice. For first-time learners, guided training provides the structured, step-by-step instruction they need before practising independently.

c.label: Simulation app — throw them into a realistic scenario so they learn by doing
c.feedback: Too early. Without knowing the correct procedure, students will make errors without understanding why. Guided training should come before open simulation.

n2.prompt: Students have completed the guided training. Several still made errors on some steps. What is the best next step?

a.label: Move to simulation — let them practise the full assessment with consequences for their decisions
a.feedback: Good progression. After learning the steps through guided training, simulation provides the realistic practice environment where mistakes have visible consequences, deepening learning.

b.label: Repeat the guided training — they need more practice with the structured steps
b.feedback: Some repetition can help, but at some point students need to practise without scaffolding. Simulation provides the transfer opportunity that repetitive guided training cannot.

c.label: Skip to evaluation — test them immediately to see who passes
c.feedback: Premature. Students who just learned the steps need practice time in simulation before being assessed. Evaluation should come after sufficient practice.

n3.prompt: You have built a learning path: guided training first, then simulation for practice. Your students are now performing patient assessments in VR with visible consequences for their decisions.
n3.endFeedback: Well done. You followed the most effective sequence: guided training teaches the steps, then simulation proves students know them. This guided → simulation progression is the foundation of effective VR-based skill training.

scenario.context: A teacher uses a VR firefighting simulation with students. The simulation is realistic — students must locate exits, use extinguishers, and evacuate. Students had an exciting time and talked about it all day. However, when asked to explain what they learned about fire safety procedures, none could articulate the steps clearly.
scenario.question: What went wrong?

choices.a: The simulation was too exciting — entertainment overrode learning
choices.b: The teacher skipped the briefing and debriefing — without a clear learning objective beforehand and structured reflection afterward, the experience was engaging but not educational
choices.c: Simulation apps are not effective for learning — the teacher should have used a video instead

feedback.a: Excitement is not the problem — it is the lack of structure around the experience. Simulations need clear objectives set in advance and reflection afterward to convert excitement into learning.
feedback.b: Correct. Without a briefing setting the learning objective and a debriefing connecting the experience to knowledge, even the most powerful simulation becomes just an exciting event. Structure turns experience into learning.
feedback.c: Simulations are highly effective for skill-based learning. The issue here is pedagogical structure, not the medium.

c1.context: A vocational school offers two VR apps for electrical wiring: App A guides students through each wire connection step by step with immediate feedback. App B places students in a realistic scenario where they must wire a circuit from memory — incorrect wiring causes a visible short circuit.
c1.question: What type is each app, and when should each be used?

choices.a: App A is guided training (for first-time learning), App B is simulation (for practice after learning the steps) — use A first, then B
choices.b: Both are simulations — they just have different difficulty levels
choices.c: App B is better in all cases because students learn more from making mistakes

feedback.a: Correct. Guided training teaches the steps; simulation proves you know them. The most effective approach uses guided training first, then simulation for reinforcement.
feedback.b: They serve fundamentally different purposes. App A provides scaffolding and instruction; App B provides open practice with consequences. These are distinct app types.
feedback.c: Making mistakes is valuable, but only after students know the correct procedure. Without guided training first, students in simulation do not know what they are doing wrong or why.

c2.context: A medical school uses a VR simulation where nursing students assess a virtual patient. The simulation tracks response time, assessment completeness, and decision accuracy. After the simulation, the instructor reviews each student's performance data.
c2.question: What additional function does this simulation serve beyond practice?

choices.a: It only serves as practice — simulations cannot evaluate
choices.b: It also functions as an evaluation tool — the performance tracking provides objective data on competency, combining simulation with assessment
choices.c: It replaces the need for any real-world clinical practice

feedback.a: Many simulations include built-in performance tracking, which adds an evaluation dimension. The data produced can serve both formative and summative assessment purposes.
feedback.b: Correct. When a simulation tracks and reports learner performance, it combines practice with evaluation. The instructor gets objective data on each student's competency.
feedback.c: VR simulation supplements but does not replace real-world practice. It provides a safe environment for initial practice and assessment before real clinical exposure.

s1.statement: In simulation apps, mistakes have visible consequences — which is what makes them effective for learning.
s1.explanation: True. The ability to fail safely and see consequences is the core advantage of simulation-based VR training.

s2.statement: Students should always start with simulation apps before trying guided training.
s2.explanation: False. The most effective sequence is guided training first (to learn the steps), then simulation (to practise without scaffolding).

s3.statement: A simulation app can serve both as a practice tool and as an evaluation tool if it tracks learner performance.
s3.explanation: True. When simulations record performance data, they combine practice with assessment, giving instructors objective evidence of competency.

b4.title: Choosing the Right App for Your Objective

exercise.instruction: Arrange the four VR app types in the correct learning sequence — from first exposure to final assessment.
exercise.i1: Exploration — orient the learner to the environment and build context
exercise.i2: Guided training — teach the correct procedure step by step with feedback
exercise.i3: Simulation — practise without scaffolding, experiencing consequences
exercise.i4: Evaluation — verify competency with tracked, objective performance data

scenario.context: A vocational welding school has three VR apps available: an exploration of a professional welding workshop, a step-by-step guided welding tutorial with feedback, and a welding simulation where students must complete joints that are tested for structural integrity.
scenario.question: In what order should students use these apps?

choices.a: Simulation first — students learn best from hands-on practice, even without prior instruction
choices.b: Exploration first (to understand the workshop environment), then guided tutorial (to learn the correct technique), then simulation (to practise independently)
choices.c: It does not matter — students can use them in any order and achieve the same result

feedback.a: Starting with simulation before students know the correct technique leads to unstructured errors. Students need to learn the steps first through guided training.
feedback.b: Correct. This follows the ideal learning sequence: orient → instruct → practise. Each stage builds on the previous one, creating a complete learning path.
feedback.c: Sequence matters significantly. Jumping to simulation before guided training means students do not know the correct procedure, and skipping exploration means they lack environmental context.

c1.context: A language teacher wants students to practise job interview conversations in VR. Students need to respond to interview questions from a virtual interviewer, receive feedback on their answers, and improve through multiple attempts.
c1.question: Which app type matches this objective?

choices.a: A communication/conversational app — it provides dialogue-based interaction with virtual characters, branching conversations, and feedback on communication skills
choices.b: A simulation app — because students are practising a real-world skill
choices.c: An evaluation app — because it assesses student performance

feedback.a: Correct. Communication apps specialise in dialogue-based interaction with realistic virtual characters. They are purpose-built for soft skills training like interviews, customer service, and interpersonal communication.
feedback.b: While interview practice involves real-world skills, communication apps are a specialised category designed specifically for conversational interaction, not physical simulation.
feedback.c: Evaluation apps focus on measuring competency, not practising it. The teacher wants students to practise and improve, which is a training function.

c2.context: A history teacher wants students to explore a virtual reconstruction of ancient Rome. There are no tasks, no quiz, and no structured activity — the teacher just wants students to get a sense of the environment before a lecture on Roman architecture.
c2.question: Which app type is this, and is it appropriate for this purpose?

choices.a: It is a simulation — students are simulating being in ancient Rome
choices.b: It is a guided training app — students are being trained on Roman history
choices.c: It is an exploration app — and it is appropriate for building context before the lecture, as long as the teacher pairs it with a focus question or observation task

feedback.a: Simulation requires interaction with consequences. Passively exploring an environment without performing tasks is exploration, not simulation.
feedback.b: Guided training requires a fixed sequence of steps with feedback. Free exploration of an environment is not guided training.
feedback.c: Correct. Exploration apps orient learners to an environment. Using it before a lecture for context-building is a valid use case — but always pair it with a specific task to avoid the 'museum effect' of passive touring.

cls.instruction: Match each learning scenario with the most appropriate VR app type.
cls.i1: Students tour a virtual archaeological site before studying ancient civilizations
cls.i2: Nursing students practise triage decisions with realistic patient deterioration
cls.i3: New technicians follow step-by-step instructions to assemble a circuit board
cls.i4: Geography students navigate a virtual rainforest to observe biodiversity
cls.i5: Pilots practise emergency landing procedures with instrument failures
cls.i6: Lab assistants learn the correct sequence for handling hazardous materials

exercise.instruction: Sort each evaluation question into the correct checklist category: Pedagogical, Technical, or Classroom.
exercise.i1: What specifically does the student learn from this app?
exercise.i2: What does the student actively do inside the app?
exercise.i3: What feedback does the app provide on learner actions?
exercise.i4: How long does the app take to load and launch?
exercise.i5: Is the interface intuitive or does it require extensive explanation?
exercise.i6: Is the app stable — does it crash, lag, or overheat the headset?
exercise.i7: What is the real duration including setup and transitions?
exercise.i8: How many students can use the app simultaneously?
exercise.i9: What is your Plan B if the technology fails during class?

scenario.context: A colleague recommends a VR anatomy app, saying 'the online reviews are great — 4.8 stars!' You have a class scheduled tomorrow and are considering using the app without testing it yourself first.
scenario.question: Should you use it untested based on the reviews?

choices.a: Yes — 4.8 stars is a strong indicator of quality, and there is no time to test
choices.b: No — online reviews cannot reveal how the app performs under your specific classroom conditions. Always test apps yourself on the same hardware before using them with students.
choices.c: Maybe — use it tomorrow as a trial run and evaluate it based on how students react

feedback.a: Reviews reflect other users' experiences in other contexts. They cannot tell you about load times, space requirements, casting compatibility, or pedagogical gaps in your specific setup.
feedback.b: Correct. Online reviews are no substitute for hands-on testing in your own classroom conditions. Technical issues, pedagogical gaps, and practical constraints can only be discovered by testing yourself.
feedback.c: Using students as test subjects for untested technology risks wasting their learning time. If the app crashes or does not work as expected, you have no Plan B prepared.

c1.context: A teacher tests a VR chemistry lab app before class. The app loads quickly, the interface is intuitive, and it runs smoothly without crashes. The teacher declares it ready for classroom use.
c1.question: Has the teacher completed a thorough evaluation?

choices.a: No — they only checked the technical dimension. They still need to evaluate the pedagogical dimension (what students learn, what they do, what feedback the app gives) and the classroom dimension (duration, capacity, Plan B).
choices.b: Yes — if the app works technically, it is ready to use
choices.c: Almost — they just need to check if casting works

feedback.a: Correct. A thorough evaluation covers three dimensions: pedagogical, technical, and classroom. Passing the technical check is necessary but not sufficient.
feedback.b: Technical stability is just one of three dimensions. An app that works perfectly but teaches nothing or cannot handle your class size is still not classroom-ready.
feedback.c: Casting is one item on the classroom checklist, but there are many more classroom and pedagogical questions that remain unanswered.

c2.context: A VR app for electrical safety training scores well on the pedagogical checklist: clear objectives, active learner tasks, feedback, and measurable outcomes. However, during testing, it takes 4 minutes to load on each headset, requires a large room-scale play area, and frequently overheats the headset after 10 minutes.
c2.question: Should this app be used in class?

choices.a: No — not without modifications to the technical setup. The pedagogical quality is strong, but the technical issues will consume classroom time and cause disruptions.
choices.b: Yes — the pedagogical quality is excellent, and technical issues can be solved on the spot during class
choices.c: Only if you have a large room and powerful headsets

feedback.a: Not yet — but it is not a rejection. The pedagogical quality makes this app worth pursuing. Work on the technical issues: pre-load the app before class, plan for the space requirement, and schedule shorter sessions to avoid overheating.
feedback.b: Correct. The pedagogical quality justifies the effort, but the technical issues need planned solutions — not improvised fixes during class. Pre-load apps, arrange space, and plan for cooling breaks.
feedback.c: Having the right hardware is part of the solution, but the 4-minute load time and overheating issues require procedural solutions regardless of equipment quality.

s1.statement: Every VR app should be tested by the instructor in classroom conditions before being used with students.
s1.explanation: True. Hands-on testing in the same conditions students will face is the only way to discover technical, pedagogical, and practical issues before they affect learning.

s2.statement: If a VR app has high ratings in the Meta Store, it does not need to be tested before classroom use.
s2.explanation: False. Store ratings reflect other contexts and priorities. They cannot predict how the app will perform under your specific classroom conditions.

s3.statement: A complete app evaluation covers three dimensions: pedagogical, technical, and classroom.
s3.explanation: True. All three dimensions must pass. An app that fails in any one dimension will create problems during the session.

exercise.instruction: Drag the correct words into the blanks to complete the VR lesson integration framework.
exercise.template: Before the VR session, provide a clear __blank1__ that states the __blank2__ and explains what students will do. During VR, __blank3__ student progress and use __blank4__ to observe from outside the headset. After VR, run a structured __blank5__ to connect the experience to real-world practice.

scenario.context: A teacher uses a VR chemistry lab app for 30 minutes. There was no briefing — students were told 'just open the app.' There was no debriefing — students removed headsets and moved to the next subject. Two days later, no student can recall the experimental procedure they performed in VR.
scenario.question: What was the main mistake?

choices.a: The VR session was too long — 30 minutes exceeds the recommended time
choices.b: The app was used in isolation without the before-during-after structure — no briefing set a purpose, no monitoring ensured engagement, and no debriefing connected the experience to learning
choices.c: The chemistry lab app was the wrong choice — a video would have been more effective

feedback.a: Session length was not the core problem. Even a 10-minute session without briefing and debriefing would produce the same result — engagement without retention.
feedback.b: Correct. Without a briefing, students had no focus. Without monitoring, the teacher could not guide the experience. Without a debriefing, the experience was not processed into knowledge. All three stages are essential.
feedback.c: The app itself was not the problem — the absence of pedagogical structure around it was. Any tool, including video, requires a before-during-after framework.

c1.context: A teacher plans a VR session using a fire evacuation simulation. She writes this briefing: 'Today you will use a VR fire evacuation simulation. Your goal is to evacuate all occupants within 3 minutes using the correct protocol. Pay attention to the order in which you check rooms and how you communicate with virtual occupants.'
c1.question: Is this an effective briefing?

choices.a: No — it reveals too much information and spoils the experience
choices.b: Yes — it is clear, sets a specific objective, tells students what to do, and defines what success looks like, all in under 60 seconds
choices.c: It is too detailed — a simple 'do your best' would be sufficient

feedback.a: A good briefing should clarify purpose, not create mystery. Students need to know what they are doing and what to focus on — this is orientation, not spoilers.
feedback.b: Correct. This briefing follows the best practice: clear objective, specific task, and success criteria. It focuses students without being unnecessarily long.
feedback.c: Vague instructions like 'do your best' provide no focus. Students need to know the specific objective and what to pay attention to.

c2.context: After a VR patient assessment simulation, a teacher asks these debriefing questions: 'What happened during your assessment? What decision was the hardest? What would you do differently next time? How does this connect to real clinical practice?'
c2.question: Is this an effective debriefing?

choices.a: Yes — it follows a structured progression from describing the experience, to analysing decisions, to connecting to real-world practice
choices.b: No — it asks too many questions and will take too long
choices.c: No — debriefing should only ask whether students enjoyed the experience

feedback.a: Correct. These questions follow a natural debriefing progression: recall (what happened), analysis (what was hard, what would change), and transfer (real-world connection). This structure consolidates VR experiences into lasting knowledge.
feedback.b: Four focused questions can be covered in 5-10 minutes. The depth of reflection is more important than speed — skipping debriefing entirely would be the time-wasting choice.
feedback.c: Enjoyment feedback is not debriefing. Structured debriefing processes the experience into knowledge through reflection, analysis, and real-world connection.

cls.instruction: Classify each action into the correct phase of VR lesson integration.
cls.i1: State the learning objective and explain what students will do
cls.i2: Use casting to observe student experiences from outside the headset
cls.i3: Ask students what they would do differently next time
cls.i4: Test the app yourself on the same hardware students will use
cls.i5: Assist students who encounter technical difficulties
cls.i6: Connect the VR experience to real-world practice through discussion

quizStep.title: Module quiz
quizStep.subtitle: Answer all questions and score at least 70% to pass.

bootcamp.stepLocked: Complete the previous step to unlock

q1.question: Which of the following best describes what happens when a student uses Virtual Reality?
q1.a: Digital elements are added on top of the real classroom
q1.b: The real environment is fully replaced by a computer-generated one — the student can no longer see the physical classroom
q1.c: The student still sees the real classroom with virtual elements overlaid that they can interact with
q1.d: The student plays a 3D video game displayed on a television
q1.explanation: VR replaces reality entirely. The student is placed inside a 360° digital environment and can't see the physical classroom. Option (a) describes AR, (c) describes MR, and (d) is a traditional video game — none of those are VR.

q2.question: You want students to practice an emergency evacuation procedure without any real danger. Which strength of VR makes this the right tool?
q2.a: VR headsets are wireless, so students can move around freely
q2.b: VR keeps students motivated because it's a novel and entertaining experience
q2.c: VR provides a safe practice environment where students can make mistakes and repeat the procedure without real-world consequences
q2.d: VR lets you record the session for grading later
q2.explanation: One of VR's core strengths is safe repetition. Students can practice dangerous or costly procedures multiple times without real consequences. Don't confuse novelty (b) with pedagogical value — the engagement wears off after a few sessions, but safe practice keeps teaching.

q3.question:
A colleague describes two classroom activities. Which technology is each one?
Activity 1 — Students point their tablet at a physical skeleton model and see 3D labels appear over the real bones.
Activity 2 — Students put on headsets and explore the inside of a human cell as if walking through it.
q3.a: Both are VR because they both use digital content
q3.b: Activity 1 is AR (digital overlays on the real world), Activity 2 is VR (replaces reality with a digital environment)
q3.c: Activity 1 is VR, Activity 2 is AR
q3.d: Both are AR because they both involve 3D content
q3.explanation: AR adds digital content on top of the real world — students still see the skeleton and classroom. VR replaces reality entirely — students are inside a digital cell and can't see the classroom. The key question: is the real world still visible?
q3.imageAlt: Two classroom scenes side by side: on the left a student holds a tablet over a skeleton model and sees 3D labels over the real bones; on the right a student wearing a VR headset explores the inside of a human cell as if they were inside it.

q4.question: A colleague says: 'I used VR in my history class — students watched a 360° video of ancient Rome for 15 minutes.' How would you evaluate this use of VR?
q4.a: Excellent — 360° videos are the best way to use VR in education
