choices.c: 10–15 minutes — enough time for a complete assessment attempt while staying within the optimal attention window

feedback.a: 30 minutes in VR causes attention drops, eye strain, and diminishing returns. After 15 minutes, cognitive focus drops sharply. Multiple shorter sessions are more effective than one long one.
feedback.b: 5 minutes may not be enough for a complete trauma assessment. Students need time to work through the full procedure. 10–15 minutes balances depth with attention capacity.
feedback.c: Correct. 10–15 minutes provides enough time for a complete assessment attempt while keeping students in the optimal cognitive engagement window. Shorter sessions can be repeated for practice.

s6.context: Step 5: During the simulation, a student has been standing still for 2 minutes, staring at the patient without acting.

choices.a: Offer a brief, specific prompt: 'Start with the airway — check if the patient is breathing.' Then step back and let them continue.
choices.b: Give detailed instructions for the entire assessment sequence
choices.c: Let them figure it out — struggling is learning

feedback.a: Correct. A minimal prompt unblocks the student without doing the task for them. One specific nudge ('Start with the airway') is enough to get them moving while preserving autonomy.
feedback.b: Giving the full procedure defeats the purpose of simulation. Students need to recall and apply the steps themselves. A small nudge is enough.
feedback.c: Struggling with a challenge is learning. But standing still for 2+ minutes means the student is stuck, not struggling. A brief prompt prevents frustration while keeping the learning experience productive.

s7.context: Step 6: The simulation is over. Headsets are off. Time to debrief. A student says 'That was intense.' Others nod.

choices.a: Say 'Great!' and move to the next topic
choices.b: Show a video of the correct procedure
choices.c: Ask: 'Walk me through your first action at the scene. What did you check first? What did you find? If you were called to a real accident tomorrow, what would you do the same — and what would you change?'

feedback.a: 'Intense' is an emotional response, not learning. Without structured reflection, students remember the feeling but not the lessons. Always follow up with specific questions.
feedback.b: Showing the correct answer turns debriefing into a lecture. Students learn more by reconstructing their own decisions than by being told what they should have done.
feedback.c: Excellent debriefing approach. You start with recall (what did you do), move to analysis (what worked, what didn't), and end with transfer (what would you do in real life). This extracts maximum learning from the experience.

s8.context: Step 7: During debriefing, a student says: 'I panicked when I saw the blood and forgot the assessment sequence completely.'

choices.a: Tell them they failed and need to study more
choices.b: Normalise it: 'That's exactly why we practice in VR — so you can experience that panic in a safe environment. Next time, you'll remember to start with the airway check because you've now felt what it's like to forget.' Schedule another practice session.
choices.c: Avoid discussing the panic — focus only on what went right

feedback.a: Calling it failure discourages the student. VR is a safe practice environment — the whole point is to make mistakes without consequences. Use the mistake as a learning moment.
feedback.b: Perfect response. You've normalised the experience (panic is natural), shown the value of VR practice (safe environment for mistakes), identified the lesson (start with airway), and offered a path forward (another session). This is supportive, constructive debriefing.
feedback.c: The panic IS the learning opportunity. By addressing it directly, you help the student develop strategies for managing stress in real situations. Avoiding it wastes a valuable teachable moment.

s9.context: Step 8: After several practice sessions, you want to confirm students are ready for their clinical placement. Which activity type do you use?

choices.a: Another Guided Exploration of the accident scene
choices.b: Evaluation / Skill Check — students perform the full trauma assessment under exam conditions: no prompts, no guidance, scored on accuracy, sequence, timing, and decision quality.
choices.c: A written theory exam about trauma assessment

feedback.a: Students already know the environment from multiple sessions. More exploration adds nothing at this stage. They need to demonstrate competence.
feedback.b: Correct. After sufficient practice, Evaluation / Skill Check is the appropriate final step. It confirms that students can perform independently under realistic conditions — the last checkpoint before real clinical work.
feedback.c: A written exam tests recall, not performance. Paramedics need to demonstrate the physical and cognitive skills in context. VR assessment is more authentic than a paper test for procedural skills.

faqs.metaDesc: Frequently asked questions about using VR Education Hub and integrating VR into education.
faqs.title: FAQs
faqs.subtitle: Quick answers to common questions. More items will be added as the platform evolves.
faqs.a1: Not yet. The Bootcamp is under development and will be released in stages.
faqs.q2: Where can I find technical requirements for VR hardware?
faqs.a2: Use the Documentation section to browse hardware and setup topics, including recommended specs and troubleshooting.
faqs.q3: Can I switch the site language?
faqs.a3: Yes. Use the language switcher in the top-right corner to change between English and Spanish.
faqs.q4: Who is VR Education Hub for?
faqs.a4: VR Education Hub is aimed at teachers, trainers, and educators at any level who want to introduce or improve the use of virtual reality in their teaching. No prior VR experience is required; the documentation and Bootcamp are designed for beginners and those looking to deepen their practice.
faqs.a5: Yes. The documentation and learning resources on VR Education Hub are free to use. Our goal is to make VR in education accessible to as many educators as possible.
faqs.q6: What age is VR suitable for in the classroom?
faqs.a6: Recommendations vary by headset and app. Many classroom VR solutions are designed for students aged 13 and older, but some experiences are suitable for younger learners with supervision. Check the Documentation section for age guidelines and safety best practices specific to your hardware and use case.
faqs.q7: How do I search the documentation?
faqs.a7: On the Documentation page you can type in the search box to filter modules by text (titles and content). You can also use the Categories filter in the sidebar to narrow results by topic (e.g. VR Fundamentals, Hardware & Setup, Pedagogical Design, Implementation).
faqs.q8: How often is the content updated?
faqs.a8: We update the documentation as new modules are ready and when hardware or best practices change. The Bootcamp will be released in stages. Check back periodically or browse the Documentation for the latest guides and recommendations.
